**How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10 without xvfb?**
Type: Solution

URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7128989

Content: How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10 without xvfb? Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. Content: How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10 without xvfb? Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Content: How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10 without xvfb? Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. Content: How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10 without xvfb? Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. Details Each major version (X.0) of Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time is supported for a period of ten years after the initial release (aka general availability or GA). Releases and Support Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time may include new features for the first three years from general availability. After that three year period, only Critical and Important Security errata advisories (RHSAs) and Urgent Priority Bug Fix errata advisories (RHBAs) will be released as they become available. All updates, upgrades and feature enhancements are provided at Red Hat's discretion on an if and when available basis. Extended Lifecycle Support (ELS) is not available with Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time. The RHEL-RT (Real-Time) is available with standard RHEL entitlements beginning with RHEL 9.0 General Availability (GA). However, the support policy changes starting with RHEL 9.6 and 10.0: Even-numbered RHEL-RT streams (e.g., 9.6, 9.8, 10.0, 10.2) support a 4-year Enhanced Extended Update Support (EUS) period. Odd-numbered streams (e.g., 9.7, 9.9, 10.1) are only supported until the release of the next even-numbered stream (e.g., 9.7 is supported until 9.8 is released). **How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7056059
Last updated: 2025-11-14
Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

**How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7056059
Last updated: 2025-11-14
Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

**How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7056059
Last updated: 2025-11-14
Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time is a separate and unique subscription that is distinct from a subscription for Red Hat Enterprise Linux although some technology is common to both. Details Each major version (X.0) of Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time is supported for a period of ten years after the initial release (aka general availability or GA). Releases and Support Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time may include new features for the first three years from general availability. After that three year period, only Critical and Important Security errata advisories (RHSAs) and Urgent Priority Bug Fix errata advisories (RHBAs) will be released as they become available. All updates, upgrades and feature enhancements are provided at Red Hat's discretion on an if and when available basis. Extended Lifecycle Support (ELS) is not available with Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time. The RHEL-RT (Real-Time) is available with standard RHEL entitlements beginning with RHEL 9.0 General Availability (GA). However, the support policy changes starting with RHEL 9.6 and 10.0: Even-numbered RHEL-RT streams (e.g., 9.6, 9.8, 10.0, 10.2) support a 4-year Enhanced Extended Update Support (EUS) period. Odd-numbered streams (e.g., 9.7, 9.9, 10.1) are only supported until the release of the next even-numbered stream (e.g., 9.7 is supported until 9.8 is released). **How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7056059
Last updated: 2025-11-14
Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time is a separate and unique subscription that is distinct from a subscription for Red Hat Enterprise Linux although some technology is common to both. Details Each major version (X.0) of Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time is supported for a period of ten years after the initial release (aka general availability or GA). Releases and Support Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time may include new features for the first three years from general availability. After that three year period, only Critical and Important Security errata advisories (RHSAs) and Urgent Priority Bug Fix errata advisories (RHBAs) will be released as they become available. All updates, upgrades and feature enhancements are provided at Red Hat's discretion on an if and when available basis. Extended Lifecycle Support (ELS) is not available with Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time. The RHEL-RT (Real-Time) is available with standard RHEL entitlements beginning with RHEL 9.0 General Availability (GA). However, the support policy changes starting with RHEL 9.6 and 10.0: Even-numbered RHEL-RT streams (e.g., 9.6, 9.8, 10.0, 10.2) support a 4-year Enhanced Extended Update Support (EUS) period. Odd-numbered streams (e.g., 9.7, 9.9, 10.1) are only supported until the release of the next even-numbered stream (e.g., 9.7 is supported until 9.8 is released). Releases and Support Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time may include new features for the first three years from general availability. After that three year period, only Critical and Important Security errata advisories (RHSAs) and Urgent Priority Bug Fix errata advisories (RHBAs) will be released as they become available. All updates, upgrades and feature enhancements are provided at Red Hat's discretion on an if and when available basis. Extended Lifecycle Support (ELS) is not available with Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time. The RHEL-RT (Real-Time) is available with standard RHEL entitlements beginning with RHEL 9.0 General Availability (GA). However, the support policy changes starting with RHEL 9.6 and 10.0: Even-numbered RHEL-RT streams (e.g., 9.6, 9.8, 10.0, 10.2) support a 4-year Enhanced Extended Update Support (EUS) period. Odd-numbered streams (e.g., 9.7, 9.9, 10.1) are only supported until the release of the next even-numbered stream (e.g., 9.7 is supported until 9.8 is released). Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

These combinations extend many applications’ useful life cycles and ease upgrade concerns. Red Hat Support may provide differentiated support between “fully compatible” and “workload specific” configurations (described below). Please discuss this with your support associate. Guidelines for Container Compatibility In specific use cases, Red Hat recommends aligning the major version of the RHEL-based container image and the RHEL container host. Red Hat also recommends following the Red Hat Container Support Policy because other container engines and runtimes are not supported. In RHEL versions 8 and later, Red Hat provides container images for each of the supported architectures (Intel/Amd x86, Arm AArch64, IBM POWER, and zSeries). In general, the correct hardware architecture will automatically be detected, and the correct image will be used. ... The container image and host architectures must match for container images to run properly and be supported. The container host’s hardware must also meet the minimum hardware requirements for the container image in order to run the image correctly (e.g. RHEL 9 container images for IBM POWER LE require POWER9 hardware, RHEL 10 container images for x86_64 require x86-64-v3). There are known limitations when running a newer container image on an older container host and while this configuration often works, such a configuration can introduce additional risk and compatibility problems. If customers run into these limitations, Red Hat recommends upgrading the container host. When containers run with This content is not included. privilege (ex. podman run --privileged) and/or modify the underlying host, Red Hat only supports matched major versions between the container image and container host (ex. RHEL 10 image on a RHEL 10 host). This recommendation also applies when containers are granted security privileges to interact with other containers for the purposes of inspection or monitoring. Aligning the major versions of the image and host ensures the highest degree of compatibility and eliminates many of the edge-cases that users encounter when mixing different versions of container images (userspace) with container hosts (kernels). Finally, customers may run into challenges when running extremely old images on newer container hosts (ex. RHEL 6 container image on RHEL 9 or 10 host). In these situations Red Hat recommends using a version supported in the table above (e.g. RHEL 8, RHEL 9 or RHEL 10 image on a RHEL 9 host.). Detailed Support Conditions The configurations defined below do not supersede the Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10: Application Compatibility Guide and support for Red Hat software provided within a container image is still governed by those guidelines. ... Root Cause When doing a leapp upgrade from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 there is no warning or upgraded is prevented from proceeding forward when there are pacemaker resources configured that are no longer supported on RHEL 10 or no longer included in RHEL 10 controld , Filesystem resources for gfs2 filesystems. Content: Why does Leapp upgrade not prevent upgrades from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 when there are gfs2 Filesystems configured? Solution Unverified - Updated 24 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Pacemaker cluster Filesystem resource agents using fstype=gfs2 Issue Why does leapp upgrade not prevented when upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 when there are gfs2 Filesystems configured? Resolution Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 This issue is being tracked with the issue This content is not included. RHEL-64281 ( Create LEAPP actor for Pacemaker 9 -> 10 upgrades ) for RHEL 9. Root Cause When doing a leapp upgrade from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 there is no warning or upgraded is prevented from proceeding forward when there are pacemaker resources configured that are no longer supported on RHEL 10 or no longer included in RHEL 10 controld , Filesystem resources for gfs2 filesystems. The issue is that the leapp upgrade should either not continue with upgrade or provide some type of warning to the end user when it finds unsupported resources configured for pacemaker . The resources controld , Filesystems with fstype=gfs2 , or lvmlockd are no longer supported on RHEL 10. For more information see: Support Policies for RHEL Resilient Storage - RHEL High Availability Requirements for Resilient Storage Resilient Storage Add-On will be discontinued with RHEL 10 NOTE: The leapp upgrade should be successful, but once pacemaker is started on the RHEL 10 cluster nodes those resource-agents that are no longer supported on RHEL 10 will fail to start. Check your binaries and search for a libdb requirement, for example: $ find /usr/bin /usr/local/bin -type f -executable -exec ldd {} + 2>/dev/null | grep libdb Locate your database’s files. Use the file utility to determine the type of the database file. Select your future database backend. For a list of possible replacements, see the Red Hat Knowledgebase solution Available replacements for the deprecated Berkeley DB (libdb) . ... Run db_converter, for example: $ db_converter --src access.db --dest access.gdbm Adapt your application for the GDBM database backend. Alternatively, the libdb package is available in the unsupported EPEL 10 repository. Last updated: 2026-02-24
Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy Purpose This document describes how Red Hat provides support for components of Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI (RHEL AI). Support of individual components is limited to use of RHEL AI via the RHEL AI user interface (UI), command line interface (CLI), and application programming interface (API) when deployed via the provided RHEL AI images. We do not support direct use of individual components. Additional software installed by the customer is unsupported as described in the Red Hat support policy on third party software . Specific technologies shipped with RHEL AI are: Granite : an open source, Apache 2 licensed foundation model from IBM. RHEL Image Mode (bootc) : RHEL AI is distributed as a “bootable container” image. These RHEL AI appliance images are provisioned via a kickstart enabled installer (downloaded as an iso, raw, or qcow2 image) onto bare metal or cloud instances. vLLM : A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs, based on PyTorch. GPU Enablement : Drivers and packages to provide GPU based acceleration. Granite Only the models listed in the official product documentation are supported. Depending on the release version, some models may be released as Technology Preview . Please see RHEL AI documentation for supported models for your version. Granite models are open source with support via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . Use of other models, specifically non-generative chat models, is not supported. Image Mode for RHEL Image mode is supported under GA terms. The Red Hat Container Support Policy outlines policy specific to containers. vLLM vLLM components provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . GPU Enablement These packages are distributed by Red Hat in the RHEL AI images. GPU enablement packages provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via the typical support through access.redhat.com . Red Hat will engage vendors as needed for additional support of these packages.

Overview Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 7 RHEA-2019:3190 introduced RHEL System Roles for SAP to assist with remotely or locally configuring a RHEL system for the installation of SAP HANA or SAP NetWeaver software. RHEL System Roles for SAP development is based on the Content from sap-linuxlab.github.io is not included. SAP LinuxLab upstream project. RHEL System Roles is a collection of roles executed by Ansible to assist administrators with RHEL system configuration. These roles are provided in the RHEL AppStream repository. In contrast, RHEL System Roles for SAP is provided in the RHEL for SAP Solutions subscription. The Red Hat Enterprise Linux for SAP Solution subscription provides support for RHEL System Roles for SAP with Ansible Core. However, if you require full support for Ansible Core, a separate subscription is necessary. Additional information is available at Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) System Roles . The following RHEL System Roles for SAP are fully supported on control nodes running RHEL 8.4 and later: sap_general_preconfigure sap_netweaver_preconfigure sap_hana_preconfigure sap_hana_install The following RHEL System Roles for SAP are supported as Technology Preview on control nodes running RHEL 8.4 and later: sap_ha_install_hana_hsr sap_ha_pacemaker_cluster sap_swpm (*) The RHEL System Roles for SAP, just like the RHEL System Roles , are installed and run from a central node referred to as the control node (which can be Content from www.ansible.com is not included. Ansible Automation controller , This content is not included. Red Hat Satellite , or a RHEL 9 or RHEL 8 host). The control node connects to the local host and/or to one or more remote hosts (called managed nodes in the context of Ansible), and performs installation and configuration steps on them. It is recommended that you use the latest major release of RHEL on the control node (RHEL 9) and use the latest version of the roles either from the rhel-system-roles-sap RPM or from This content is not included. Red Hat Automation Hub . The RHEL System Roles for SAP and Ansible packages do not need to be installed on the systems that are being managed/configured. This document describes how to install and use RHEL package rhel-system-roles-sap version 3.3.0 (*) or Automation Hub collection redhat.sap_install version 1.2.1, which consists of the following roles. **Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7101311
Last updated: 2025-01-23
Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. Type: Documentation
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/support/policy/updates/rhelai/policy
Last updated: 2026-02-24
Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy Purpose This document describes how Red Hat provides support for components of Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI (RHEL AI). Support of individual components is limited to use of RHEL AI via the RHEL AI user interface (UI), command line interface (CLI), and application programming interface (API) when deployed via the provided RHEL AI images. We do not support direct use of individual components. Additional software installed by the customer is unsupported as described in the Red Hat support policy on third party software . Specific technologies shipped with RHEL AI are: Granite : an open source, Apache 2 licensed foundation model from IBM. RHEL Image Mode (bootc) : RHEL AI is distributed as a “bootable container” image. These RHEL AI appliance images are provisioned via a kickstart enabled installer (downloaded as an iso, raw, or qcow2 image) onto bare metal or cloud instances. vLLM : A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs, based on PyTorch. GPU Enablement : Drivers and packages to provide GPU based acceleration. Granite Only the models listed in the official product documentation are supported. Depending on the release version, some models may be released as Technology Preview . Please see RHEL AI documentation for supported models for your version. Granite models are open source with support via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . Use of other models, specifically non-generative chat models, is not supported. Image Mode for RHEL Image mode is supported under GA terms. The Red Hat Container Support Policy outlines policy specific to containers. vLLM vLLM components provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . GPU Enablement These packages are distributed by Red Hat in the RHEL AI images. GPU enablement packages provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via the typical support through access.redhat.com . Red Hat will engage vendors as needed for additional support of these packages.

**Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL

Last updated: 2025-01-23
Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. **Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy**
Type: Documentation
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/support/policy/updates/rhelai/policy
Last updated: 2026-02-24
Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy Purpose This document describes how Red Hat provides support for components of Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI (RHEL AI). Support of individual components is limited to use of RHEL AI via the RHEL AI user interface (UI), command line interface (CLI), and application programming interface (API) when deployed via the provided RHEL AI images. We do not support direct use of individual components. Additional software installed by the customer is unsupported as described in the Red Hat support policy on third party software . Specific technologies shipped with RHEL AI are: Granite : an open source, Apache 2 licensed foundation model from IBM. RHEL Image Mode (bootc) : RHEL AI is distributed as a “bootable container” image. These RHEL AI appliance images are provisioned via a kickstart enabled installer (downloaded as an iso, raw, or qcow2 image) onto bare metal or cloud instances. vLLM : A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs, based on PyTorch. GPU Enablement : Drivers and packages to provide GPU based acceleration. Granite Only the models listed in the official product documentation are supported. Depending on the release version, some models may be released as Technology Preview . Please see RHEL AI documentation for supported models for your version. Granite models are open source with support via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . Use of other models, specifically non-generative chat models, is not supported. Image Mode for RHEL Image mode is supported under GA terms. The Red Hat Container Support Policy outlines policy specific to containers. vLLM vLLM components provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . GPU Enablement These packages are distributed by Red Hat in the RHEL AI images. GPU enablement packages provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via the typical support through access.redhat.com . Red Hat will engage vendors as needed for additional support of these packages.

**Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ?**
Type: Solution

Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. Type: Documentation
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/support/policy/updates/rhelai/policy
Last updated: 2026-02-24
Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy Purpose This document describes how Red Hat provides support for components of Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI (RHEL AI). Support of individual components is limited to use of RHEL AI via the RHEL AI user interface (UI), command line interface (CLI), and application programming interface (API) when deployed via the provided RHEL AI images. We do not support direct use of individual components. Additional software installed by the customer is unsupported as described in the Red Hat support policy on third party software . Specific technologies shipped with RHEL AI are: Granite : an open source, Apache 2 licensed foundation model from IBM. RHEL Image Mode (bootc) : RHEL AI is distributed as a “bootable container” image. These RHEL AI appliance images are provisioned via a kickstart enabled installer (downloaded as an iso, raw, or qcow2 image) onto bare metal or cloud instances. vLLM : A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs, based on PyTorch. GPU Enablement : Drivers and packages to provide GPU based acceleration. Granite Only the models listed in the official product documentation are supported. Depending on the release version, some models may be released as Technology Preview . Please see RHEL AI documentation for supported models for your version. Granite models are open source with support via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . Use of other models, specifically non-generative chat models, is not supported. Image Mode for RHEL Image mode is supported under GA terms. The Red Hat Container Support Policy outlines policy specific to containers. vLLM vLLM components provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . GPU Enablement These packages are distributed by Red Hat in the RHEL AI images. GPU enablement packages provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via the typical support through access.redhat.com . Red Hat will engage vendors as needed for additional support of these packages.

The control node connects to the local host and/or to one or more remote hosts (called managed nodes in the context of Ansible), and performs installation and configuration steps on them. **Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ?**
Type: Solution

Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. Last updated: 2026-02-24
Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy Purpose This document describes how Red Hat provides support for components of Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI (RHEL AI). Support of individual components is limited to use of RHEL AI via the RHEL AI user interface (UI), command line interface (CLI), and application programming interface (API) when deployed via the provided RHEL AI images. We do not support direct use of individual components. Additional software installed by the customer is unsupported as described in the Red Hat support policy on third party software . Specific technologies shipped with RHEL AI are: Granite : an open source, Apache 2 licensed foundation model from IBM. RHEL Image Mode (bootc) : RHEL AI is distributed as a “bootable container” image. These RHEL AI appliance images are provisioned via a kickstart enabled installer (downloaded as an iso, raw, or qcow2 image) onto bare metal or cloud instances. vLLM : A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs, based on PyTorch. GPU Enablement : Drivers and packages to provide GPU based acceleration. Granite Only the models listed in the official product documentation are supported. Depending on the release version, some models may be released as Technology Preview . Please see RHEL AI documentation for supported models for your version. Granite models are open source with support via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . Use of other models, specifically non-generative chat models, is not supported. Image Mode for RHEL Image mode is supported under GA terms. The Red Hat Container Support Policy outlines policy specific to containers. vLLM vLLM components provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . GPU Enablement These packages are distributed by Red Hat in the RHEL AI images. GPU enablement packages provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via the typical support through access.redhat.com . Red Hat will engage vendors as needed for additional support of these packages.

The control node connects to the local host and/or to one or more remote hosts (called managed nodes in the context of Ansible), and performs installation and configuration steps on them. Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. URL: https://access.redhat.com/support/policy/updates/rhelai/policy
Last updated: 2026-02-24
Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy Purpose This document describes how Red Hat provides support for components of Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI (RHEL AI). Support of individual components is limited to use of RHEL AI via the RHEL AI user interface (UI), command line interface (CLI), and application programming interface (API) when deployed via the provided RHEL AI images. We do not support direct use of individual components. Additional software installed by the customer is unsupported as described in the Red Hat support policy on third party software . Specific technologies shipped with RHEL AI are: Granite : an open source, Apache 2 licensed foundation model from IBM. RHEL Image Mode (bootc) : RHEL AI is distributed as a “bootable container” image. These RHEL AI appliance images are provisioned via a kickstart enabled installer (downloaded as an iso, raw, or qcow2 image) onto bare metal or cloud instances. vLLM : A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs, based on PyTorch. GPU Enablement : Drivers and packages to provide GPU based acceleration. Granite Only the models listed in the official product documentation are supported. Depending on the release version, some models may be released as Technology Preview . Please see RHEL AI documentation for supported models for your version. Granite models are open source with support via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . Use of other models, specifically non-generative chat models, is not supported. Image Mode for RHEL Image mode is supported under GA terms. The Red Hat Container Support Policy outlines policy specific to containers. vLLM vLLM components provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . GPU Enablement These packages are distributed by Red Hat in the RHEL AI images. GPU enablement packages provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via the typical support through access.redhat.com . Red Hat will engage vendors as needed for additional support of these packages.

The control node connects to the local host and/or to one or more remote hosts (called managed nodes in the context of Ansible), and performs installation and configuration steps on them. It is recommended that you use the latest major release of RHEL on the control node (RHEL 9) and use the latest version of the roles either from the rhel-system-roles-sap RPM or from This content is not included. Red Hat Automation Hub . The RHEL System Roles for SAP and Ansible packages do not need to be installed on the systems that are being managed/configured. This document describes how to install and use RHEL package rhel-system-roles-sap version 3.3.0 (*) or Automation Hub collection redhat.sap_install version 1.2.1, which consists of the following roles. Note Package rhel-system-roles-sap version 3.3.0 does not contain the role sap_swpm . This role is planned to be released in the next version of package rhel-system-roles-sap . Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. query: What DHCP server options are available in RHEL 10?
response:
In Red Hat Enterprise Linux, the Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) server functionality is typically provided by the Internet Systems Consortium (ISC) DHCP server. While the search results specifically reference RHEL 5 and RHEL 6 documentation, the core components and configuration practices for DHCP servers have generally remained consistent across RHEL versions, assuming no explicit deprecation for RHEL 10 has been announced regarding the ISC DHCP server.

To configure a DHCP server in RHEL, you would generally:

1.  **Install the `dhcp` package:** This package provides the necessary server daemon (`dhcpd`).
2.  **Configure the server:** The primary configuration file is `/etc/dhcpd.conf`. You can find a sample configuration file at `/usr/share/doc/dhcp-<version>/dhcpd.conf.sample` (replace `<version>` with the installed DHCP package version). This file allows you to define options such as:
    *   Subnets to manage
    *   IP address ranges for leases
    *   Default lease times and maximum lease times
    *   DNS servers to provide to clients
    *   Domain names
    *   Gateway addresses
    *   Host-specific fixed IP addresses
3.  **Manage the lease database:** DHCP uses a lease database, typically located at `/var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases`, to store information about assigned IP addresses and their lease times.

For multi-homed DHCP servers, you can configure host-specific options within the `dhcpd.conf` file. DHCPv6 is also supported for IPv6 environments, with its own configuration mechanisms, similar to DHCPv4.

**Sources:**
- [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5 Deployment Guide: Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP)](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/5/html/deployment_guide/s1-dhcp-configuring-server)
- [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 Deployment Guide: DHCP Servers](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/6/html/deployment_guide/sect-configuring_a_multihomed_dhcp_server)

Content: Python 3.11 support has been dropped. Target/Managed Nodes (minimum Python 3.9): Python 3.9 is now the minimum supported version for managed/target nodes. Python 3.8 support has been dropped, and Python 3.14 has been added. RHEL Compatibility Matrix: The following table shows Python availability across Red Hat Enterprise Linux releases and compatibility with Ansible Core 2.20 requirements: RHEL Version Default Python Additional Python Available Control Node (min 3.12) Target Node (min 3.9) RHEL 8 3.6 3.9 (module streams); 3.11, 3.12 (AppStream) ✅ Python 3.12 via dnf install python3.12 ✅ Python 3.9 via dnf module install python39 RHEL 9 3.9 3.11 (since 9.2); 3.12 (since 9.4) ✅ Python 3.12 via dnf install python3.12 (RHEL 9.4+) ✅ Default Python 3.9 meets requirement RHEL 10 3.12 Additional versions via AppStream ✅ Default Python 3.12 meets requirement ✅ Default Python 3.12 meets requirement Key Considerations: RHEL 10 is fully compatible out of the box for both control and target node roles, with Python 3.12 as the system default. RHEL 9.4+ meets the control node requirement when Python 3.12 is installed from AppStream (dnf install python3.12). The system default Python 3.9 is sufficient for the target node role. Customers running RHEL 9.0 through 9.3 will need to upgrade to at least RHEL 9.4 to obtain Python 3.12 for the control node. RHEL 8 can serve as both a control node and target node, but requires installing non-default Python versions from AppStream. Mandatory locking causes the kernel to check every open, read, and write to verify that the calling process isn’t violating a lock on the given file. query: What DHCP server options are available in RHEL 10?
response:
In Red Hat Enterprise Linux, the Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) server functionality is typically provided by the Internet Systems Consortium (ISC) DHCP server. While the search results specifically reference RHEL 5 and RHEL 6 documentation, the core components and configuration practices for DHCP servers have generally remained consistent across RHEL versions, assuming no explicit deprecation for RHEL 10 has been announced regarding the ISC DHCP server.

To configure a DHCP server in RHEL, you would generally:

1.  **Install the `dhcp` package:** This package provides the necessary server daemon (`dhcpd`).
2.  **Configure the server:** The primary configuration file is `/etc/dhcpd.conf`. You can find a sample configuration file at `/usr/share/doc/dhcp-<version>/dhcpd.conf.sample` (replace `<version>` with the installed DHCP package version). This file allows you to define options such as:
    *   Subnets to manage
    *   IP address ranges for leases
    *   Default lease times and maximum lease times
    *   DNS servers to provide to clients
    *   Domain names
    *   Gateway addresses
    *   Host-specific fixed IP addresses
3.  **Manage the lease database:** DHCP uses a lease database, typically located at `/var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases`, to store information about assigned IP addresses and their lease times.

For multi-homed DHCP servers, you can configure host-specific options within the `dhcpd.conf` file. DHCPv6 is also supported for IPv6 environments, with its own configuration mechanisms, similar to DHCPv4.

**Sources:**
- [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5 Deployment Guide: Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP)](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/5/html/deployment_guide/s1-dhcp-configuring-server)
- [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 Deployment Guide: DHCP Servers](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/6/html/deployment_guide/sect-configuring_a_multihomed_dhcp_server)

Content: As the platform moves toward a container-first model, the RPM-based installer is being deprecated in the Ansible Automation Platform 2.5 release and removed in a future release. In future releases, customers will have the option to use the operator if they are OpenShift users or the containerized installer that supports deployments on RHEL. Deployment topologies Red Hat tests Ansible Automation Platform 2.5 with a defined set of topologies to provide you with opinionated deployment options. While it is possible to install the Ansible Automation Platform on different infrastructure topologies and with different environment configurations, Red Hat guarantees support for the topologies outlined in the following table. At the time of the Ansible Automation Platform 2.5 GA release, a limited set of topologies are fully tested. Red Hat will regularly add new topologies to iteratively expand the scope of fully tested deployment options. As new topologies roll out, we will include them in the This content is not included. Tested deployment models for Ansible Automation Platform article. The following table outlines the different ways to install or deploy Ansible Automation Platform 2.5: Mode Infrastructure Description Tested topologies RPM Virtual Machines / Bare Metal The RPM installer deploys the Ansible Automation Platform on Red Hat Enterprise Linux using RPMs to install the platform on host machines. ... Automation controller, automation hub All non-local authentications into the automation controller and automation hub are deprecated. Use the platform gateway to configure external authentications, such as SAML, LDAP, and RADIUS. Ansible-core The INI configuration option in COLLECTIONS_PATHS is deprecated. Use the singular form COLLECTIONS_PATH instead. Ansible-core The environment variable ANSIBLE_COLLECTIONS_PATHS is deprecated. Use the singular form ANSIBLE_COLLECTIONS_PATH instead. Content: How to enable Mandatory Locking using "mand" option Solution Verified - Updated 29 May 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue How to enable Mandatory Locking using mand option in Red Hat Enterprise Linux? Why server is not able to use mand mount option in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8? mand mount option can be used in RHEL 7 and 9 but it is giving error in RHEL8. Resolution Mandatory locking is deprecated in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 or later . ... Mandatory locking causes the kernel to check every open, read, and write to verify that the calling process isn’t violating a lock on the given file. To enable mandatory locking in Linux, you need to enable it on a file system level, and also on the individual files. The steps to be followed are: Mount the file system with mand option. For the lock_file, turn on the set-group-ID bit and turn off the group-execute bit, to enable mandatory locking on that particular file. (This way has been chosen because when group-execute bit is turned on, set-group-ID has no real meaning to it) Root Cause To enable mandatory locking in Linux, it has to be enabled at file system level, and also on the individual files. The mand option is deprecated in RHEL8 or later. ... See BUGS below. Because of these bugs, and the fact that the feature is believed to be little used, since Linux 4.5, mandatory locking has been made an optional feature, governed by a configuration option (CON‐ FIG_MANDATORY_FILE_LOCKING). This feature is no longer supported at all in Linux 5.15 and above. 11.1. File systems of Considerations in adopting RHEL 9 describes the feature as below. Mandatory file locking support has been removed from RHEL 9Mandatory file locking is no longer supported in RHEL 9 and later versions. query: What DHCP server options are available in RHEL 10?
response:
In Red Hat Enterprise Linux, the Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) server functionality is typically provided by the Internet Systems Consortium (ISC) DHCP server. While the search results specifically reference RHEL 5 and RHEL 6 documentation, the core components and configuration practices for DHCP servers have generally remained consistent across RHEL versions, assuming no explicit deprecation for RHEL 10 has been announced regarding the ISC DHCP server.

To configure a DHCP server in RHEL, you would generally:

1.  **Install the `dhcp` package:** This package provides the necessary server daemon (`dhcpd`).
2.  **Configure the server:** The primary configuration file is `/etc/dhcpd.conf`. You can find a sample configuration file at `/usr/share/doc/dhcp-<version>/dhcpd.conf.sample` (replace `<version>` with the installed DHCP package version). This file allows you to define options such as:
    *   Subnets to manage
    *   IP address ranges for leases
    *   Default lease times and maximum lease times
    *   DNS servers to provide to clients
    *   Domain names
    *   Gateway addresses
    *   Host-specific fixed IP addresses
3.  **Manage the lease database:** DHCP uses a lease database, typically located at `/var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases`, to store information about assigned IP addresses and their lease times.

For multi-homed DHCP servers, you can configure host-specific options within the `dhcpd.conf` file. DHCPv6 is also supported for IPv6 environments, with its own configuration mechanisms, similar to DHCPv4.

**Sources:**
- [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5 Deployment Guide: Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP)](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/5/html/deployment_guide/s1-dhcp-configuring-server)
- [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 Deployment Guide: DHCP Servers](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/6/html/deployment_guide/sect-configuring_a_multihomed_dhcp_server)

Why server is not able to use mand mount option in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8? mand mount option can be used in RHEL 7 and 9 but it is giving error in RHEL8. Resolution Mandatory locking is deprecated in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 or later . ... Mandatory locking causes the kernel to check every open, read, and write to verify that the calling process isn’t violating a lock on the given file. To enable mandatory locking in Linux, you need to enable it on a file system level, and also on the individual files. The steps to be followed are: Mount the file system with mand option. For the lock_file, turn on the set-group-ID bit and turn off the group-execute bit, to enable mandatory locking on that particular file. (This way has been chosen because when group-execute bit is turned on, set-group-ID has no real meaning to it) Root Cause To enable mandatory locking in Linux, it has to be enabled at file system level, and also on the individual files. The mand option is deprecated in RHEL8 or later. ... See BUGS below. Because of these bugs, and the fact that the feature is believed to be little used, since Linux 4.5, mandatory locking has been made an optional feature, governed by a configuration option (CON‐ FIG_MANDATORY_FILE_LOCKING). query: What DHCP server options are available in RHEL 10?
response:
In Red Hat Enterprise Linux, the Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) server functionality is typically provided by the Internet Systems Consortium (ISC) DHCP server. While the search results specifically reference RHEL 5 and RHEL 6 documentation, the core components and configuration practices for DHCP servers have generally remained consistent across RHEL versions, assuming no explicit deprecation for RHEL 10 has been announced regarding the ISC DHCP server.

To configure a DHCP server in RHEL, you would generally:

1.  **Install the `dhcp` package:** This package provides the necessary server daemon (`dhcpd`).
2.  **Configure the server:** The primary configuration file is `/etc/dhcpd.conf`. You can find a sample configuration file at `/usr/share/doc/dhcp-<version>/dhcpd.conf.sample` (replace `<version>` with the installed DHCP package version). This file allows you to define options such as:
    *   Subnets to manage
    *   IP address ranges for leases
    *   Default lease times and maximum lease times
    *   DNS servers to provide to clients
    *   Domain names
    *   Gateway addresses
    *   Host-specific fixed IP addresses
3.  **Manage the lease database:** DHCP uses a lease database, typically located at `/var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases`, to store information about assigned IP addresses and their lease times.

For multi-homed DHCP servers, you can configure host-specific options within the `dhcpd.conf` file. DHCPv6 is also supported for IPv6 environments, with its own configuration mechanisms, similar to DHCPv4.

**Sources:**
- [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5 Deployment Guide: Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP)](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/5/html/deployment_guide/s1-dhcp-configuring-server)
- [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 Deployment Guide: DHCP Servers](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/6/html/deployment_guide/sect-configuring_a_multihomed_dhcp_server)

Resolution Mandatory locking is deprecated in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 or later . ... Mandatory locking causes the kernel to check every open, read, and write to verify that the calling process isn’t violating a lock on the given file. To enable mandatory locking in Linux, you need to enable it on a file system level, and also on the individual files. The steps to be followed are: Mount the file system with mand option. For the lock_file, turn on the set-group-ID bit and turn off the group-execute bit, to enable mandatory locking on that particular file. (This way has been chosen because when group-execute bit is turned on, set-group-ID has no real meaning to it) Root Cause To enable mandatory locking in Linux, it has to be enabled at file system level, and also on the individual files. query: What DHCP server options are available in RHEL 10?
response:
In Red Hat Enterprise Linux, the Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) server functionality is typically provided by the Internet Systems Consortium (ISC) DHCP server. While the search results specifically reference RHEL 5 and RHEL 6 documentation, the core components and configuration practices for DHCP servers have generally remained consistent across RHEL versions, assuming no explicit deprecation for RHEL 10 has been announced regarding the ISC DHCP server.

To configure a DHCP server in RHEL, you would generally:

1.  **Install the `dhcp` package:** This package provides the necessary server daemon (`dhcpd`).
2.  **Configure the server:** The primary configuration file is `/etc/dhcpd.conf`. You can find a sample configuration file at `/usr/share/doc/dhcp-<version>/dhcpd.conf.sample` (replace `<version>` with the installed DHCP package version). This file allows you to define options such as:
    *   Subnets to manage
    *   IP address ranges for leases
    *   Default lease times and maximum lease times
    *   DNS servers to provide to clients
    *   Domain names
    *   Gateway addresses
    *   Host-specific fixed IP addresses
3.  **Manage the lease database:** DHCP uses a lease database, typically located at `/var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases`, to store information about assigned IP addresses and their lease times.

For multi-homed DHCP servers, you can configure host-specific options within the `dhcpd.conf` file. DHCPv6 is also supported for IPv6 environments, with its own configuration mechanisms, similar to DHCPv4.

**Sources:**
- [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5 Deployment Guide: Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP)](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/5/html/deployment_guide/s1-dhcp-configuring-server)
- [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 Deployment Guide: DHCP Servers](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/6/html/deployment_guide/sect-configuring_a_multihomed_dhcp_server)

Why server is not able to use mand mount option in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8? mand mount option can be used in RHEL 7 and 9 but it is giving error in RHEL8. Resolution Mandatory locking is deprecated in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 or later . ... Mandatory locking causes the kernel to check every open, read, and write to verify that the calling process isn’t violating a lock on the given file. To enable mandatory locking in Linux, you need to enable it on a file system level, and also on the individual files. The steps to be followed are: Mount the file system with mand option. For the lock_file, turn on the set-group-ID bit and turn off the group-execute bit, to enable mandatory locking on that particular file. (This way has been chosen because when group-execute bit is turned on, set-group-ID has no real meaning to it) Root Cause To enable mandatory locking in Linux, it has to be enabled at file system level, and also on the individual files. The mand option is deprecated in RHEL8 or later. ... See BUGS below. Because of these bugs, and the fact that the feature is believed to be little used, since Linux 4.5, mandatory locking has been made an optional feature, governed by a configuration option (CON‐ FIG_MANDATORY_FILE_LOCKING). This feature is no longer supported at all in Linux 5.15 and above. 11.1. File systems of Considerations in adopting RHEL 9 describes the feature as below. query: What DHCP server options are available in RHEL 10?
response:
In Red Hat Enterprise Linux, the Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) server functionality is typically provided by the Internet Systems Consortium (ISC) DHCP server. While the search results specifically reference RHEL 5 and RHEL 6 documentation, the core components and configuration practices for DHCP servers have generally remained consistent across RHEL versions, assuming no explicit deprecation for RHEL 10 has been announced regarding the ISC DHCP server.

To configure a DHCP server in RHEL, you would generally:

1.  **Install the `dhcp` package:** This package provides the necessary server daemon (`dhcpd`).
2.  **Configure the server:** The primary configuration file is `/etc/dhcpd.conf`. You can find a sample configuration file at `/usr/share/doc/dhcp-<version>/dhcpd.conf.sample` (replace `<version>` with the installed DHCP package version). This file allows you to define options such as:
    *   Subnets to manage
    *   IP address ranges for leases
    *   Default lease times and maximum lease times
    *   DNS servers to provide to clients
    *   Domain names
    *   Gateway addresses
    *   Host-specific fixed IP addresses
3.  **Manage the lease database:** DHCP uses a lease database, typically located at `/var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases`, to store information about assigned IP addresses and their lease times.

For multi-homed DHCP servers, you can configure host-specific options within the `dhcpd.conf` file. DHCPv6 is also supported for IPv6 environments, with its own configuration mechanisms, similar to DHCPv4.

**Sources:**
- [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5 Deployment Guide: Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP)](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/5/html/deployment_guide/s1-dhcp-configuring-server)
- [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 Deployment Guide: DHCP Servers](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/6/html/deployment_guide/sect-configuring_a_multihomed_dhcp_server)

Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Content: How to enable Mandatory Locking using "mand" option Solution Verified - Updated 29 May 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue How to enable Mandatory Locking using mand option in Red Hat Enterprise Linux? Why server is not able to use mand mount option in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8? mand mount option can be used in RHEL 7 and 9 but it is giving error in RHEL8. Resolution Mandatory locking is deprecated in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 or later . ... Mandatory locking causes the kernel to check every open, read, and write to verify that the calling process isn’t violating a lock on the given file. To enable mandatory locking in Linux, you need to enable it on a file system level, and also on the individual files. The steps to be followed are: Mount the file system with mand option. For the lock_file, turn on the set-group-ID bit and turn off the group-execute bit, to enable mandatory locking on that particular file. (This way has been chosen because when group-execute bit is turned on, set-group-ID has no real meaning to it) Root Cause To enable mandatory locking in Linux, it has to be enabled at file system level, and also on the individual files. The mand option is deprecated in RHEL8 or later. ... See BUGS below. Because of these bugs, and the fact that the feature is believed to be little used, since Linux 4.5, mandatory locking has been made an optional feature, governed by a configuration option (CON‐ FIG_MANDATORY_FILE_LOCKING). This feature is no longer supported at all in Linux 5.15 and above. 11.1. File systems of Considerations in adopting RHEL 9 describes the feature as below. Mandatory file locking support has been removed from RHEL 9Mandatory file locking is no longer supported in RHEL 9 and later versions. The mand mount option is ignored by this kernel and its use generates a warning in the system log. The mand option does work in RHEL7. Diagnostic Steps File system can be mounted with mand option in RHEL7 and 9 but it is not getting mounted in RHEL8 as option mand is deprecated in RHEL8. In RHEL9, the mand option can be ginve, but is ignored. In this support model, support will only be provided on the latest release. If a non-security related bug is discovered in the current version, we will work to address it in the next scheduled release. The life cycle of each release and the release cadence will be selected based on what makes sense for the product. Products covered by this support policy adhere to the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Life Cycle errata criteria for the Production Phase of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7. For example, during the Maintenance Support 2 phase, the tools will receive Red Hat-defined Critical and Important impact Security Advisories (RHSAs) in the current release and selected (at Red Hat discretion) Urgent Priority Bug Fix Advisories (RHBAs) in the next or last scheduled release. ... Please see the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Life Cycle page for more information. These products will not have a corresponding Extended Update Support (EUS) nor an Extended Life Cycle Support (ELS) offering. In this support model, support will only be provided on the latest release. If a non-security related bug is discovered in the current version, we will work to address it in the next scheduled release. The life cycle of each release and the release cadence will be selected based on what makes sense for the product. Products covered by this support policy adhere to the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Life Cycle errata criteria for the Production Phase of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7. For example, during the Maintenance Support 2 phase, the tools will receive Red Hat-defined Critical and Important impact Security Advisories (RHSAs) in the current release and selected (at Red Hat discretion) Urgent Priority Bug Fix Advisories (RHBAs) in the next or last scheduled release. ... Please see the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Life Cycle page for more information. These products will not have a corresponding Extended Update Support (EUS) nor an Extended Life Cycle Support (ELS) offering.
