Customers are expected to upgrade Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI to the most current supported version of the product in a timely fashion. Bug fixes and features are targeted for the latest versions of the product (Full Support Phase), See below for more information on Production Phases. Lifecycle Dates Lifecycle Phases The life cycle for Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI is currently limited to a single Full Support phase. Full Support Full support is provided according to the published Scope of Coverage and Service Level Agreement. Likewise, Development Support is provided according to the published Scope of Coverage and Service Level Agreement.During the Full Support Phase, qualified Critical and Important Security Advisories (RHSAs) and Urgent and Selected High Priority Bug Fix advisories (RHBAs) will be released as they become available; all other available fix and qualified patches may be released via periodic updates. Customers are expected to upgrade their Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI environment to the most current supported version. On request, and at Red Hat’s discretion, qualified Critical Security errata advisories (RHSAs) and Critical Bug Fix errata advisories (RHBAs) may be made available for non-current minor versions. Content: Red Hat Developer Tools for Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7 Support Policy Support Policy The Red Hat Developer Tools utilize a rolling release support model, which is best suited for fast-evolving products like these tools. In this support model, support will only be provided on the latest release. If a non-security related bug is discovered in the current version, we will work to address it in the next scheduled release. The life cycle of each release and the release cadence will be selected based on what makes sense for the product. Products covered by this support policy adhere to the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Life Cycle errata criteria for the Production Phase of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7. For example, during the Maintenance Support 2 phase, the tools will receive Red Hat-defined Critical and Important impact Security Advisories (RHSAs) in the current release and selected (at Red Hat discretion) Urgent Priority Bug Fix Advisories (RHBAs) in the next or last scheduled release. ... Please see the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Life Cycle page for more information. These products will not have a corresponding Extended Update Support (EUS) nor an Extended Life Cycle Support (ELS) offering. Content: Red Hat Developer Tools for Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7 Support Policy Support Policy The Red Hat Developer Tools utilize a rolling release support model, which is best suited for fast-evolving products like these tools. In this support model, support will only be provided on the latest release. If a non-security related bug is discovered in the current version, we will work to address it in the next scheduled release. The life cycle of each release and the release cadence will be selected based on what makes sense for the product. Products covered by this support policy adhere to the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Life Cycle errata criteria for the Production Phase of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7. For example, during the Maintenance Support 2 phase, the tools will receive Red Hat-defined Critical and Important impact Security Advisories (RHSAs) in the current release and selected (at Red Hat discretion) Urgent Priority Bug Fix Advisories (RHBAs) in the next or last scheduled release. ... Please see the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Life Cycle page for more information. These products will not have a corresponding Extended Update Support (EUS) nor an Extended Life Cycle Support (ELS) offering. query: How do I migrate my DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10?
response:
Migrating a DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 generally involves upgrading the operating system and then reconfiguring or migrating the DHCP server configuration. Red Hat recommends an in-place upgrade using the `leapp` utility for upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10.

Here's a general approach:

1.  **Plan the Upgrade:**
    *   Review the official Red Hat documentation for upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10, paying close attention to system requirements, limitations, and potential incompatibilities.
    *   Consider testing the upgrade process in a non-production environment first.

2.  **Backup Existing DHCP Configuration:**
    *   Before performing any upgrade, back up your current DHCP server configuration files, typically located in `/etc/dhcp/` (e.g., `dhcpd.conf`).
    *   Also, back up any lease files (e.g., `/var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases`).

3.  **Perform the RHEL In-place Upgrade:**
    *   Utilize the `leapp` utility to perform the in-place upgrade from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10. This tool helps assess upgradability and manages the upgrade process. The general steps involve:
        *   Updating the RHEL 9 system to the latest minor release.
        *   Installing the `leapp` utility and its data.
        *   Running `leapp preupgrade` to identify potential issues and generate a pre-upgrade report. Address any blocking issues reported.
        *   Performing the upgrade using `leapp upgrade`.
        *   Rebooting the system to the RHEL 10 kernel.

4.  **Reconfigure DHCP on RHEL 10:**
    *   After the RHEL 10 upgrade is complete, install the DHCP server package if it's not already present:
        ```bash
        sudo dnf install dhcp-server
        ```
    *   Restore your backed-up `dhcpd.conf` and `dhcpd.leases` files to their respective locations (`/etc/dhcp/` and `/var/lib/dhcpd/`).
    *   Review and adjust the DHCP configuration file (`dhcpd.conf`) for any changes or new features introduced in the RHEL 10 version of the DHCP server.
    *   Start and enable the DHCP service:
        ```bash
        sudo systemctl start dhcpd
        sudo systemctl enable dhcpd
        ```
    *   Verify the DHCP server's functionality and ensure clients are receiving IP addresses correctly.

**Important Considerations:**

*   The `leapp` utility is the recommended tool for in-place upgrades between major RHEL versions.
*   Always ensure you have comprehensive backups before starting any major system upgrade.

query: How do I migrate my DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10?
response:
Migrating a DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 generally involves upgrading the operating system and then reconfiguring or migrating the DHCP server configuration. Red Hat recommends an in-place upgrade using the `leapp` utility for upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10.

Here's a general approach:

1.  **Plan the Upgrade:**
    *   Review the official Red Hat documentation for upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10, paying close attention to system requirements, limitations, and potential incompatibilities.
    *   Consider testing the upgrade process in a non-production environment first.

2.  **Backup Existing DHCP Configuration:**
    *   Before performing any upgrade, back up your current DHCP server configuration files, typically located in `/etc/dhcp/` (e.g., `dhcpd.conf`).
    *   Also, back up any lease files (e.g., `/var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases`).

3.  **Perform the RHEL In-place Upgrade:**
    *   Utilize the `leapp` utility to perform the in-place upgrade from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10. This tool helps assess upgradability and manages the upgrade process. The general steps involve:
        *   Updating the RHEL 9 system to the latest minor release.
        *   Installing the `leapp` utility and its data.
        *   Running `leapp preupgrade` to identify potential issues and generate a pre-upgrade report. Address any blocking issues reported.
        *   Performing the upgrade using `leapp upgrade`.
        *   Rebooting the system to the RHEL 10 kernel.

4.  **Reconfigure DHCP on RHEL 10:**
    *   After the RHEL 10 upgrade is complete, install the DHCP server package if it's not already present:
        ```bash
        sudo dnf install dhcp-server
        ```
    *   Restore your backed-up `dhcpd.conf` and `dhcpd.leases` files to their respective locations (`/etc/dhcp/` and `/var/lib/dhcpd/`).

    *   Start and enable the DHCP service:
        ```bash
        sudo systemctl start dhcpd
        sudo systemctl enable dhcpd
        ```
    *   Verify the DHCP server's functionality and ensure clients are receiving IP addresses correctly.

**Important Considerations:**

*   The `leapp` utility is the recommended tool for in-place upgrades between major RHEL versions.
*   Always ensure you have comprehensive backups before starting any major system upgrade.

query: How do I migrate my DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10?
response:
Migrating a DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 generally involves upgrading the operating system and then reconfiguring or migrating the DHCP server configuration. Red Hat recommends an in-place upgrade using the `leapp` utility for upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10.

Here's a general approach:

1.  **Plan the Upgrade:**
    *   Review the official Red Hat documentation for upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10, paying close attention to system requirements, limitations, and potential incompatibilities.
    *   Consider testing the upgrade process in a non-production environment first.

2.  **Backup Existing DHCP Configuration:**
    *   Before performing any upgrade, back up your current DHCP server configuration files, typically located in `/etc/dhcp/` (e.g., `dhcpd.conf`).
    *   Also, back up any lease files (e.g., `/var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases`).

3.  **Perform the RHEL In-place Upgrade:**
    *   Utilize the `leapp` utility to perform the in-place upgrade from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10. This tool helps assess upgradability and manages the upgrade process. The general steps involve:
        *   Updating the RHEL 9 system to the latest minor release.
        *   Installing the `leapp` utility and its data.
        *   Running `leapp preupgrade` to identify potential issues and generate a pre-upgrade report. Address any blocking issues reported.
        *   Performing the upgrade using `leapp upgrade`.
        *   Rebooting the system to the RHEL 10 kernel.

4.  **Reconfigure DHCP on RHEL 10:**
    *   After the RHEL 10 upgrade is complete, install the DHCP server package if it's not already present:
        ```bash
        sudo dnf install dhcp-server
        ```
    *   Restore your backed-up `dhcpd.conf` and `dhcpd.leases` files to their respective locations (`/etc/dhcp/` and `/var/lib/dhcpd/`).
    *   Review and adjust the DHCP configuration file (`dhcpd.conf`) for any changes or new features introduced in the RHEL 10 version of the DHCP server.
    *   Start and enable the DHCP service:
        ```bash
        sudo systemctl start dhcpd
        sudo systemctl enable dhcpd
        ```

query: How do I migrate my DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10?
response:
Migrating a DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 generally involves upgrading the operating system and then reconfiguring or migrating the DHCP server configuration. Red Hat recommends an in-place upgrade using the `leapp` utility for upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10.

Here's a general approach:

1.  **Plan the Upgrade:**
    *   Review the official Red Hat documentation for upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10, paying close attention to system requirements, limitations, and potential incompatibilities.
    *   Consider testing the upgrade process in a non-production environment first.

2.  **Backup Existing DHCP Configuration:**
    *   Before performing any upgrade, back up your current DHCP server configuration files, typically located in `/etc/dhcp/` (e.g., `dhcpd.conf`).
    *   Also, back up any lease files (e.g., `/var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases`).

3.  **Perform the RHEL In-place Upgrade:**
    *   Utilize the `leapp` utility to perform the in-place upgrade from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10. This tool helps assess upgradability and manages the upgrade process. The general steps involve:
        *   Updating the RHEL 9 system to the latest minor release.
        *   Installing the `leapp` utility and its data.
        *   Running `leapp preupgrade` to identify potential issues and generate a pre-upgrade report. Address any blocking issues reported.
        *   Performing the upgrade using `leapp upgrade`.
        *   Rebooting the system to the RHEL 10 kernel.

4.  **Reconfigure DHCP on RHEL 10:**
    *   After the RHEL 10 upgrade is complete, install the DHCP server package if it's not already present:
        ```bash
        sudo dnf install dhcp-server
        ```
    *   Restore your backed-up `dhcpd.conf` and `dhcpd.leases` files to their respective locations (`/etc/dhcp/` and `/var/lib/dhcpd/`).
    *   Review and adjust the DHCP configuration file (`dhcpd.conf`) for any changes or new features introduced in the RHEL 10 version of the DHCP server.
    *   Start and enable the DHCP service:
        ```bash
        sudo systemctl start dhcpd
        sudo systemctl enable dhcpd
        ```
    *   Verify the DHCP server's functionality and ensure clients are receiving IP addresses correctly.

**Important Considerations:**

*   The `leapp` utility is the recommended tool for in-place upgrades between major RHEL versions.

query: How do I migrate my DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10?
response:
Migrating a DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 generally involves upgrading the operating system and then reconfiguring or migrating the DHCP server configuration. Red Hat recommends an in-place upgrade using the `leapp` utility for upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10.

Here's a general approach:

1.  **Plan the Upgrade:**
    *   Review the official Red Hat documentation for upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10, paying close attention to system requirements, limitations, and potential incompatibilities.
    *   Consider testing the upgrade process in a non-production environment first.

2.  **Backup Existing DHCP Configuration:**
    *   Before performing any upgrade, back up your current DHCP server configuration files, typically located in `/etc/dhcp/` (e.g., `dhcpd.conf`).
    *   Also, back up any lease files (e.g., `/var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases`).

3.  **Perform the RHEL In-place Upgrade:**
    *   Utilize the `leapp` utility to perform the in-place upgrade from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10. This tool helps assess upgradability and manages the upgrade process. The general steps involve:
        *   Updating the RHEL 9 system to the latest minor release.
        *   Installing the `leapp` utility and its data.
        *   Running `leapp preupgrade` to identify potential issues and generate a pre-upgrade report. Address any blocking issues reported.
        *   Performing the upgrade using `leapp upgrade`.
        *   Rebooting the system to the RHEL 10 kernel.

4.  **Reconfigure DHCP on RHEL 10:**
    *   After the RHEL 10 upgrade is complete, install the DHCP server package if it's not already present:
        ```bash
        sudo dnf install dhcp-server
        ```
    *   Restore your backed-up `dhcpd.conf` and `dhcpd.leases` files to their respective locations (`/etc/dhcp/` and `/var/lib/dhcpd/`).
    *   Review and adjust the DHCP configuration file (`dhcpd.conf`) for any changes or new features introduced in the RHEL 10 version of the DHCP server.
    *   Start and enable the DHCP service:
        ```bash
        sudo systemctl start dhcpd
        sudo systemctl enable dhcpd
        ```
    *   Verify the DHCP server's functionality and ensure clients are receiving IP addresses correctly.

**Important Considerations:**

*   The `leapp` utility is the recommended tool for in-place upgrades between major RHEL versions.
*   Always ensure you have comprehensive backups before starting any major system upgrade.

query: How do I migrate my DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10?
response:
Migrating a DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 generally involves upgrading the operating system and then reconfiguring or migrating the DHCP server configuration. Red Hat recommends an in-place upgrade using the `leapp` utility for upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10.

Here's a general approach:

1.  **Plan the Upgrade:**
    *   Review the official Red Hat documentation for upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10, paying close attention to system requirements, limitations, and potential incompatibilities.
    *   Consider testing the upgrade process in a non-production environment first.

2.  **Backup Existing DHCP Configuration:**
    *   Before performing any upgrade, back up your current DHCP server configuration files, typically located in `/etc/dhcp/` (e.g., `dhcpd.conf`).
    *   Also, back up any lease files (e.g., `/var/lib/dhcpd/dhcpd.leases`).

3.  **Perform the RHEL In-place Upgrade:**
    *   Utilize the `leapp` utility to perform the in-place upgrade from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10. This tool helps assess upgradability and manages the upgrade process. The general steps involve:
        *   Updating the RHEL 9 system to the latest minor release.
        *   Installing the `leapp` utility and its data.
        *   Running `leapp preupgrade` to identify potential issues and generate a pre-upgrade report. Address any blocking issues reported.
        *   Performing the upgrade using `leapp upgrade`.
        *   Rebooting the system to the RHEL 10 kernel.

4.  **Reconfigure DHCP on RHEL 10:**
    *   After the RHEL 10 upgrade is complete, install the DHCP server package if it's not already present:
        ```bash
        sudo dnf install dhcp-server
        ```
    *   Restore your backed-up `dhcpd.conf` and `dhcpd.leases` files to their respective locations (`/etc/dhcp/` and `/var/lib/dhcpd/`).
    *   Review and adjust the DHCP configuration file (`dhcpd.conf`) for any changes or new features introduced in the RHEL 10 version of the DHCP server.
    *   Start and enable the DHCP service:
        ```bash
        sudo systemctl start dhcpd
        sudo systemctl enable dhcpd
        ```
    *   Verify the DHCP server's functionality and ensure clients are receiving IP addresses correctly.

**Important Considerations:**

*   The `leapp` utility is the recommended tool for in-place upgrades between major RHEL versions.
*   Always ensure you have comprehensive backups before starting any major system upgrade.

query: How do I install a package on a system using rpm-ostree?
response:
I'm a Red Hat Enterprise Linux expert assistant. I can help you with RHEL-related questions. What would you like to know?

The provided documentation does not contain specific instructions for installing packages directly using `rpm-ostree install`. `rpm-ostree` is a hybrid image/package system used in immutable operating systems like Red Hat CoreOS and Fedora CoreOS. It manages the operating system as an image, and traditional package management commands like `dnf install` are not used in the same way as on a traditional RHEL system.

For layering packages on an `rpm-ostree` system, you typically use `rpm-ostree install <package_name>` to layer the package on top of the base image. This command stages a new deployment, and a reboot is usually required to apply the changes.

I recommend consulting the official Red Hat documentation for the specific RHEL variant you are using (e.g., Red Hat CoreOS) to get the most accurate and up-to-date instructions on managing packages with `rpm-ostree`.

Check your binaries and search for a libdb requirement, for example: $ find /usr/bin /usr/local/bin -type f -executable -exec ldd {} + 2>/dev/null | grep libdb Locate your database’s files. ... For a list of possible replacements, see the Red Hat Knowledgebase solution Available replacements for the deprecated Berkeley DB (libdb) . In the case of GDBM, you can use the db_converter utility included in the libdb-utils package. To install the package, enter: $ dnf install libdb-utils Backup your database. Run db_converter, for example: $ db_converter --src access.db --dest access.gdbm Adapt your application for the GDBM database backend. Alternatively, the libdb package is available in the unsupported EPEL 10 repository. query: How do I install a package on a system using rpm-ostree?
response:
I'm a Red Hat Enterprise Linux expert assistant. I can help you with RHEL-related questions. What would you like to know?

The provided documentation does not contain specific instructions for installing packages directly using `rpm-ostree install`. `rpm-ostree` is a hybrid image/package system used in immutable operating systems like Red Hat CoreOS and Fedora CoreOS. It manages the operating system as an image, and traditional package management commands like `dnf install` are not used in the same way as on a traditional RHEL system.

For layering packages on an `rpm-ostree` system, you typically use `rpm-ostree install <package_name>` to layer the package on top of the base image. This command stages a new deployment, and a reboot is usually required to apply the changes.

I recommend consulting the official Red Hat documentation for the specific RHEL variant you are using (e.g., Red Hat CoreOS) to get the most accurate and up-to-date instructions on managing packages with `rpm-ostree`.

Content: Furthermore, major releases of Red Hat Enterprise Linux contain a limited set of backward-compatible libraries included in previous major releases to allow for the easy migration of applications. Typically, Red Hat applies changes in such a way as to minimize the amount of change and to maintain binary compatibility. Exceptions may apply for controlled package re-bases under certain circumstances. The binary compatibility goal is extended to Red Hat Enterprise Linux used in application containers. Additional details can be found in the Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8: Application Compatibility Guide , Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9: Application Compatibility Guide , and the Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10: Application Compatibility Guide . There will be overlapping PostgreSQL Application Streams in RHEL 8, with each stream maintained for 5 years. Customers may select the Application Stream version that best meets their needs. They can install an older version, install a newer version and stay on it for the full 5 years, or update to the latest stream with each release, whatever best fits their use case. Customers never have to update, they can update at their own convenience. If a customer asks for support beyond the specified life of an Application Stream, they may be asked to move to a newer Application Stream to take advantage of bug fixes and CVE advisories. ... The following information has been provided by Red Hat, but is outside the scope of the posted Service Level Agreements and support procedures . Installing unsupported packages does not necessarily make a system unsupportable by Red Hat Global Support Services However, Red Hat Global Support Services will be unable to support or debug problems with packages not shipped in standard RHEL channels. ... The EPEL repository is not a part of Red Hat Enterprise Linux and does not fall under Red Hat's Production Support Scope of Coverage . The repository is considered an optional repository and is not tested by Red Hat quality engineers. Enable the codeready repos and install the CVS from the EPEL repo. For RHEL 9# subscription-manager repos --enable codeready-builder-for-rhel-9-$(arch)-rpms# dnf install https://dl.fedoraproject.org/pub/epel/epel-release-latest-9.noarch.rpm# yum install CVSFor RHEL 8# subscription-manager repos --enable codeready-builder-for-rhel-8-$(arch)-rpms# dnf install https://dl.fedoraproject.org/pub/epel/epel-release-latest-8.noarch.rpm# yum install cvs Root Cause The CVS Packqages is no longer shipped from RHEL shipped repos.

Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7106315
Last updated: 2025-02-06
Content: How to migrate from libdb to a different key-value database Solution Unverified - Updated 6 Feb 2025 Environment You use libdb on RHEL 8 or RHEL 9 and want to upgrade to RHEL 10, where libdb is no longer available. Issue The libdb package was deprecated in Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 9 and has been removed from RHEL 10. Users of libdb who want to upgrade to RHEL 10 must migrate to a different key-value database before switching to RHEL 10. Resolution RHEL 9.5 provides libdb 5.3.28 . The libdb-utils package provides a utility for conversion to GDBM database format. On the RHEL 9 host: Ensure whether migration is needed. Check your binaries and search for a libdb requirement, for example: $ find /usr/bin /usr/local/bin -type f -executable -exec ldd {} + 2>/dev/null | grep libdb Locate your database’s files. ... For a list of possible replacements, see the Red Hat Knowledgebase solution Available replacements for the deprecated Berkeley DB (libdb) . In the case of GDBM, you can use the db_converter utility included in the libdb-utils package. To install the package, enter: $ dnf install libdb-utils Backup your database. Run db_converter, for example: $ db_converter --src access.db --dest access.gdbm Adapt your application for the GDBM database backend. Alternatively, the libdb package is available in the unsupported EPEL 10 repository. query: How do I install a package on a system using rpm-ostree?
response:
I'm a Red Hat Enterprise Linux expert assistant. I can help you with RHEL-related questions. What would you like to know?

The provided documentation does not contain specific instructions for installing packages directly using `rpm-ostree install`. `rpm-ostree` is a hybrid image/package system used in immutable operating systems like Red Hat CoreOS and Fedora CoreOS. It manages the operating system as an image, and traditional package management commands like `dnf install` are not used in the same way as on a traditional RHEL system.

For layering packages on an `rpm-ostree` system, you typically use `rpm-ostree install <package_name>` to layer the package on top of the base image. This command stages a new deployment, and a reboot is usually required to apply the changes.

I recommend consulting the official Red Hat documentation for the specific RHEL variant you are using (e.g., Red Hat CoreOS) to get the most accurate and up-to-date instructions on managing packages with `rpm-ostree`.

Content: How to migrate from libdb to a different key-value database Solution Unverified - Updated 6 Feb 2025 Environment You use libdb on RHEL 8 or RHEL 9 and want to upgrade to RHEL 10, where libdb is no longer available. Issue The libdb package was deprecated in Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 9 and has been removed from RHEL 10. Users of libdb who want to upgrade to RHEL 10 must migrate to a different key-value database before switching to RHEL 10. Resolution RHEL 9.5 provides libdb 5.3.28 . The libdb-utils package provides a utility for conversion to GDBM database format. On the RHEL 9 host: Ensure whether migration is needed. Check your binaries and search for a libdb requirement, for example: $ find /usr/bin /usr/local/bin -type f -executable -exec ldd {} + 2>/dev/null | grep libdb Locate your database’s files. ... For a list of possible replacements, see the Red Hat Knowledgebase solution Available replacements for the deprecated Berkeley DB (libdb) . In the case of GDBM, you can use the db_converter utility included in the libdb-utils package. To install the package, enter: $ dnf install libdb-utils Backup your database. Run db_converter, for example: $ db_converter --src access.db --dest access.gdbm Adapt your application for the GDBM database backend. Alternatively, the libdb package is available in the unsupported EPEL 10 repository. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

query: How do I install a package on a system using rpm-ostree?
response:
I'm a Red Hat Enterprise Linux expert assistant. I can help you with RHEL-related questions. What would you like to know?

The provided documentation does not contain specific instructions for installing packages directly using `rpm-ostree install`. `rpm-ostree` is a hybrid image/package system used in immutable operating systems like Red Hat CoreOS and Fedora CoreOS. It manages the operating system as an image, and traditional package management commands like `dnf install` are not used in the same way as on a traditional RHEL system.

For layering packages on an `rpm-ostree` system, you typically use `rpm-ostree install <package_name>` to layer the package on top of the base image. This command stages a new deployment, and a reboot is usually required to apply the changes.

I recommend consulting the official Red Hat documentation for the specific RHEL variant you are using (e.g., Red Hat CoreOS) to get the most accurate and up-to-date instructions on managing packages with `rpm-ostree`.

Content: How to migrate from libdb to a different key-value database Solution Unverified - Updated 6 Feb 2025 Environment You use libdb on RHEL 8 or RHEL 9 and want to upgrade to RHEL 10, where libdb is no longer available. Issue The libdb package was deprecated in Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 9 and has been removed from RHEL 10. Users of libdb who want to upgrade to RHEL 10 must migrate to a different key-value database before switching to RHEL 10. Resolution RHEL 9.5 provides libdb 5.3.28 . The libdb-utils package provides a utility for conversion to GDBM database format. On the RHEL 9 host: Ensure whether migration is needed. Check your binaries and search for a libdb requirement, for example: $ find /usr/bin /usr/local/bin -type f -executable -exec ldd {} + 2>/dev/null | grep libdb Locate your database’s files. ... For a list of possible replacements, see the Red Hat Knowledgebase solution Available replacements for the deprecated Berkeley DB (libdb) . In the case of GDBM, you can use the db_converter utility included in the libdb-utils package. To install the package, enter: $ dnf install libdb-utils Backup your database. Run db_converter, for example: $ db_converter --src access.db --dest access.gdbm Adapt your application for the GDBM database backend. Alternatively, the libdb package is available in the unsupported EPEL 10 repository. query: How do I install a package on a system using rpm-ostree?
response:
I'm a Red Hat Enterprise Linux expert assistant. I can help you with RHEL-related questions. What would you like to know?

The provided documentation does not contain specific instructions for installing packages directly using `rpm-ostree install`. `rpm-ostree` is a hybrid image/package system used in immutable operating systems like Red Hat CoreOS and Fedora CoreOS. It manages the operating system as an image, and traditional package management commands like `dnf install` are not used in the same way as on a traditional RHEL system.

For layering packages on an `rpm-ostree` system, you typically use `rpm-ostree install <package_name>` to layer the package on top of the base image. This command stages a new deployment, and a reboot is usually required to apply the changes.

I recommend consulting the official Red Hat documentation for the specific RHEL variant you are using (e.g., Red Hat CoreOS) to get the most accurate and up-to-date instructions on managing packages with `rpm-ostree`.

Content: How to migrate from libdb to a different key-value database Solution Unverified - Updated 6 Feb 2025 Environment You use libdb on RHEL 8 or RHEL 9 and want to upgrade to RHEL 10, where libdb is no longer available. Issue The libdb package was deprecated in Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 9 and has been removed from RHEL 10. Users of libdb who want to upgrade to RHEL 10 must migrate to a different key-value database before switching to RHEL 10. Resolution RHEL 9.5 provides libdb 5.3.28 . The libdb-utils package provides a utility for conversion to GDBM database format. On the RHEL 9 host: Ensure whether migration is needed. Check your binaries and search for a libdb requirement, for example: $ find /usr/bin /usr/local/bin -type f -executable -exec ldd {} + 2>/dev/null | grep libdb Locate your database’s files. ... For a list of possible replacements, see the Red Hat Knowledgebase solution Available replacements for the deprecated Berkeley DB (libdb) . In the case of GDBM, you can use the db_converter utility included in the libdb-utils package. To install the package, enter: $ dnf install libdb-utils Backup your database. Run db_converter, for example: $ db_converter --src access.db --dest access.gdbm Adapt your application for the GDBM database backend. Alternatively, the libdb package is available in the unsupported EPEL 10 repository. query: How do I install a package on a system using rpm-ostree?
response:
I'm a Red Hat Enterprise Linux expert assistant. I can help you with RHEL-related questions. What would you like to know?

The provided documentation does not contain specific instructions for installing packages directly using `rpm-ostree install`. `rpm-ostree` is a hybrid image/package system used in immutable operating systems like Red Hat CoreOS and Fedora CoreOS. It manages the operating system as an image, and traditional package management commands like `dnf install` are not used in the same way as on a traditional RHEL system.

For layering packages on an `rpm-ostree` system, you typically use `rpm-ostree install <package_name>` to layer the package on top of the base image. This command stages a new deployment, and a reboot is usually required to apply the changes.

I recommend consulting the official Red Hat documentation for the specific RHEL variant you are using (e.g., Red Hat CoreOS) to get the most accurate and up-to-date instructions on managing packages with `rpm-ostree`.

Content: How to migrate from libdb to a different key-value database Solution Unverified - Updated 6 Feb 2025 Environment You use libdb on RHEL 8 or RHEL 9 and want to upgrade to RHEL 10, where libdb is no longer available. Issue The libdb package was deprecated in Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 9 and has been removed from RHEL 10. Users of libdb who want to upgrade to RHEL 10 must migrate to a different key-value database before switching to RHEL 10. Resolution RHEL 9.5 provides libdb 5.3.28 . The libdb-utils package provides a utility for conversion to GDBM database format. On the RHEL 9 host: Ensure whether migration is needed. Check your binaries and search for a libdb requirement, for example: $ find /usr/bin /usr/local/bin -type f -executable -exec ldd {} + 2>/dev/null | grep libdb Locate your database’s files. ... For a list of possible replacements, see the Red Hat Knowledgebase solution Available replacements for the deprecated Berkeley DB (libdb) . In the case of GDBM, you can use the db_converter utility included in the libdb-utils package. To install the package, enter: $ dnf install libdb-utils Backup your database. Run db_converter, for example: $ db_converter --src access.db --dest access.gdbm Adapt your application for the GDBM database backend. Alternatively, the libdb package is available in the unsupported EPEL 10 repository. query: How to set up Kea?
response:
To set up Kea on Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, you will primarily configure it to provide DHCP services for IPv4 and/or IPv6 networks. Kea uses separate configuration files and systemd services for DHCPv4 and DHCPv6.

Here's a general outline of the setup process:

### 1. Install Kea

First, install the Kea package:

```bash
dnf install kea
```

### 2. Configure Kea DHCPv4 Server

1.  Edit the DHCPv4 configuration file, `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf`. Replace the example addresses and network details with those appropriate for your network.

    An example configuration for a basic DHCPv4 server might look like this (adjust `interfaces-config`, `subnet`, `pools`, `routers`, and `domain-name-servers` to match your environment):

    ```json
    {
      "Dhcp4": {
        "interfaces-config": {
          "interfaces": [ "enp1s0" ]
        },
        "subnet4": [
          {
            "id": 1,
            "subnet": "192.168.124.0/24",
            "pools": [
              {
                "pool": "192.168.124.100 - 192.168.124.200"
              }
            ],
            "option-data": [
              {
                "space": "dhcp4",
                "name": "routers",
                "code": 3,
                "data": "192.168.124.10"
              },
              {
                "space": "dhcp4",
                "name": "domain-name-servers",
                "code": 6,
                "data": "192.168.124.10"
              }
            ]
          }
        ]
      }
    }
    ```

2.  Verify the syntax of your DHCPv4 configuration:

    ```bash
    kea-dhcp4 -t /etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf
    ```

    If there are errors, correct them before proceeding.

3.  Update `firewalld` rules to allow incoming DHCPv4 traffic:

    ```bash
    firewall-cmd --permanent --add-service=dhcp
    firewall-cmd --reload
    ```

4.  Enable and start the `kea-dhcp4` service:

    ```bash
    systemctl enable --now kea-dhcp4
    ```

### 3. Configure Kea DHCPv6 Server (Optional)

If you are configuring an IPv6 network, follow these steps:

1.  Edit the DHCPv6 configuration file, `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp6.conf`. Replace the example addresses and network details with those appropriate for your IPv6 network.

    An example configuration for a basic DHCPv6 server might look like this (adjust `interfaces-config`, `subnet`, and `pools` to match your environment):

    ```json
    {
      "Dhcp6": {
        "interfaces-config": {
          "interfaces": [ "enp1s0" ]
        },
        "subnet6": [
          {
            "id": 1,
            "subnet": "fd33::/64",
            "interface": "enp1s0",
            "pools": [
              {
                "pool": "fd33::100-fd33::200"
              }
            ]
          }
        ]
      }
    }
    ```

2.  Verify the syntax of your DHCPv6 configuration:

    ```bash
    kea-dhcp6 -t /etc/kea/kea-dhcp6.conf
    ```

    If there are errors, correct them before proceeding.

3.  Update `firewalld` rules to allow incoming DHCPv6 traffic:

    ```bash
    firewall-cmd --permanent --add-service=dhcpv6
    firewall-cmd --reload
    ```

4.  Enable and start the `kea-dhcp6` service:

    ```bash
    systemctl enable --now kea-dhcp6
    ```

### 4. Verification

On a client machine configured to obtain an IP address via DHCP, check if it received an address from your Kea server:

```bash
ip address show <interface>
```

Replace `<interface>` with the client's network interface name (e.g., `enp1s0`).

### Troubleshooting

You can check on which IPv4 and IPv6 addresses Kea is listening with the command:

```bash
ss -lunp | grep -E ':(67|547)\s'
```

Sources:
- [Managing networking infrastructure services](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/managing_networking_infrastructure_services/index)
- [How to configure/setup a PXE server on RHEL 10 ?](https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7126352)

This example allows SSH for the 10.1.1.0/24 network and blocks it for all others. firewalld firewalld operates at the firewall layer and can be used to restrict access to a given service. One approach is to create a new firewall zone for the 10.1.1.0/24 network and enable SSH in the new zone. firewalld enables SSH in the default (public) zone by default. First needs to be removed from the default zone: # firewall-cmd --permanent --remove-service=ssh Create the new zone: # firewall-cmd --permanent --new-zone=mysshzone Add the SSH service and the network to the zone. # firewall-cmd --permanent --zone=mysshzone --add-source=10.1.1.0/24# firewall-cmd --permanent --zone=mysshzone --add-service=ssh Then, reload the firewall to make the new configuration active. # firewall-cmd --reload systemd systemd provides per-unit IP address allow/deny filtering. query: How to set up Kea?
response:
To set up Kea on Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, you will primarily configure it to provide DHCP services for IPv4 and/or IPv6 networks. Kea uses separate configuration files and systemd services for DHCPv4 and DHCPv6.

Here's a general outline of the setup process:

### 1. Install Kea

First, install the Kea package:

```bash
dnf install kea
```

### 2. Configure Kea DHCPv4 Server

1.  Edit the DHCPv4 configuration file, `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf`. Replace the example addresses and network details with those appropriate for your network.

    An example configuration for a basic DHCPv4 server might look like this (adjust `interfaces-config`, `subnet`, `pools`, `routers`, and `domain-name-servers` to match your environment):

    ```json
    {
      "Dhcp4": {
        "interfaces-config": {
          "interfaces": [ "enp1s0" ]
        },
        "subnet4": [
          {
            "id": 1,
            "subnet": "192.168.124.0/24",
            "pools": [
              {
                "pool": "192.168.124.100 - 192.168.124.200"
              }
            ],
            "option-data": [
              {
                "space": "dhcp4",
                "name": "routers",
                "code": 3,
                "data": "192.168.124.10"
              },
              {
                "space": "dhcp4",
                "name": "domain-name-servers",
                "code": 6,
                "data": "192.168.124.10"
              }
            ]
          }
        ]
      }
    }
    ```

2.  Verify the syntax of your DHCPv4 configuration:

    ```bash
    kea-dhcp4 -t /etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf
    ```

    If there are errors, correct them before proceeding.

3.  Update `firewalld` rules to allow incoming DHCPv4 traffic:

    ```bash
    firewall-cmd --permanent --add-service=dhcp
    firewall-cmd --reload
    ```

4.  Enable and start the `kea-dhcp4` service:

    ```bash
    systemctl enable --now kea-dhcp4
    ```

### 3. Configure Kea DHCPv6 Server (Optional)

If you are configuring an IPv6 network, follow these steps:

1.  Edit the DHCPv6 configuration file, `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp6.conf`. Replace the example addresses and network details with those appropriate for your IPv6 network.

    An example configuration for a basic DHCPv6 server might look like this (adjust `interfaces-config`, `subnet`, and `pools` to match your environment):

    ```json
    {
      "Dhcp6": {
        "interfaces-config": {
          "interfaces": [ "enp1s0" ]
        },
        "subnet6": [
          {
            "id": 1,
            "subnet": "fd33::/64",
            "interface": "enp1s0",
            "pools": [
              {
                "pool": "fd33::100-fd33::200"
              }
            ]
          }
        ]
      }
    }
    ```

2.  Verify the syntax of your DHCPv6 configuration:

    ```bash
    kea-dhcp6 -t /etc/kea/kea-dhcp6.conf
    ```

    If there are errors, correct them before proceeding.

3.  Update `firewalld` rules to allow incoming DHCPv6 traffic:

    ```bash
    firewall-cmd --permanent --add-service=dhcpv6
    firewall-cmd --reload
    ```

4.  Enable and start the `kea-dhcp6` service:

    ```bash
    systemctl enable --now kea-dhcp6
    ```

### 4. Verification

On a client machine configured to obtain an IP address via DHCP, check if it received an address from your Kea server:

```bash
ip address show <interface>
```

Replace `<interface>` with the client's network interface name (e.g., `enp1s0`).

### Troubleshooting

You can check on which IPv4 and IPv6 addresses Kea is listening with the command:

```bash
ss -lunp | grep -E ':(67|547)\s'
```

Sources:
- [Managing networking infrastructure services](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/managing_networking_infrastructure_services/index)
- [How to configure/setup a PXE server on RHEL 10 ?](https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7126352)

Do /etc/hosts.allow and /etc/hosts.deny have any use in RHEL 8? /etc/hosts.allow and /etc/hosts.deny are present on RHEL 8 Beta system. They are in the setup-2.12.2-1.el8.noarch RPM. What is the purpose now when tcp_wrappers is no longer available? How can I use the deprecated and removed tcp_wrappers in RHEL 8? Resolution firewalld can be used as a firewall level replacement for TCP Wrappers. This example allows SSH for the 10.1.1.0/24 network and blocks it for all others. firewalld firewalld operates at the firewall layer and can be used to restrict access to a given service. One approach is to create a new firewall zone for the 10.1.1.0/24 network and enable SSH in the new zone. firewalld enables SSH in the default (public) zone by default. First needs to be removed from the default zone: # firewall-cmd --permanent --remove-service=ssh Create the new zone: # firewall-cmd --permanent --new-zone=mysshzone Add the SSH service and the network to the zone. # firewall-cmd --permanent --zone=mysshzone --add-source=10.1.1.0/24# firewall-cmd --permanent --zone=mysshzone --add-service=ssh Then, reload the firewall to make the new configuration active. # firewall-cmd --reload systemd systemd provides per-unit IP address allow/deny filtering. It works with cgroup v2 .For details, see How to allow specific IP addresses or networks to access the systemd managed services Root Cause TCP Wrappers has been marked as deprecated since 2017: RHEL 7.4 Release Notes - Deprecated Functionality "tcp_wrappers deprecated" Further information is given in the upstream Fedora change to remove the feature: Content from fedoraproject.org is not included. Fedora 28 - Changes - Deprecate TCP wrappers Red Hat Bug 1663556 - /etc/hosts.allow and /etc/hosts.deny files contain extremely inaccurate information was been filed to address these files in RHEL 8.

query: How to set up Kea?
response:
To set up Kea on Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, you will primarily configure it to provide DHCP services for IPv4 and/or IPv6 networks. Kea uses separate configuration files and systemd services for DHCPv4 and DHCPv6.

Here's a general outline of the setup process:

### 1. Install Kea

First, install the Kea package:

```bash
dnf install kea
```

### 2. Configure Kea DHCPv4 Server

1.  Edit the DHCPv4 configuration file, `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf`. Replace the example addresses and network details with those appropriate for your network.

    An example configuration for a basic DHCPv4 server might look like this (adjust `interfaces-config`, `subnet`, `pools`, `routers`, and `domain-name-servers` to match your environment):

    ```json
    {
      "Dhcp4": {
        "interfaces-config": {
          "interfaces": [ "enp1s0" ]
        },
        "subnet4": [
          {
            "id": 1,
            "subnet": "192.168.124.0/24",
            "pools": [
              {
                "pool": "192.168.124.100 - 192.168.124.200"
              }
            ],
            "option-data": [
              {
                "space": "dhcp4",
                "name": "routers",
                "code": 3,
                "data": "192.168.124.10"
              },
              {
                "space": "dhcp4",
                "name": "domain-name-servers",
                "code": 6,
                "data": "192.168.124.10"
              }
            ]
          }
        ]
      }
    }
    ```

2.  Verify the syntax of your DHCPv4 configuration:

    ```bash
    kea-dhcp4 -t /etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf
    ```

    If there are errors, correct them before proceeding.

3.  Update `firewalld` rules to allow incoming DHCPv4 traffic:

    ```bash
    firewall-cmd --permanent --add-service=dhcp
    firewall-cmd --reload
    ```

4.  Enable and start the `kea-dhcp4` service:

    ```bash
    systemctl enable --now kea-dhcp4
    ```

### 3. Configure Kea DHCPv6 Server (Optional)

If you are configuring an IPv6 network, follow these steps:

1.  Edit the DHCPv6 configuration file, `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp6.conf`. Replace the example addresses and network details with those appropriate for your IPv6 network.

    An example configuration for a basic DHCPv6 server might look like this (adjust `interfaces-config`, `subnet`, and `pools` to match your environment):

    ```json
    {
      "Dhcp6": {
        "interfaces-config": {
          "interfaces": [ "enp1s0" ]
        },
        "subnet6": [
          {
            "id": 1,
            "subnet": "fd33::/64",
            "interface": "enp1s0",
            "pools": [
              {
                "pool": "fd33::100-fd33::200"
              }
            ]
          }
        ]
      }
    }
    ```

2.  Verify the syntax of your DHCPv6 configuration:

    ```bash
    kea-dhcp6 -t /etc/kea/kea-dhcp6.conf
    ```

    If there are errors, correct them before proceeding.

3.  Update `firewalld` rules to allow incoming DHCPv6 traffic:

    ```bash
    firewall-cmd --permanent --add-service=dhcpv6
    firewall-cmd --reload
    ```

4.  Enable and start the `kea-dhcp6` service:

    ```bash
    systemctl enable --now kea-dhcp6
    ```

### 4. Verification

On a client machine configured to obtain an IP address via DHCP, check if it received an address from your Kea server:

```bash
ip address show <interface>
```

Replace `<interface>` with the client's network interface name (e.g., `enp1s0`).

### Troubleshooting

You can check on which IPv4 and IPv6 addresses Kea is listening with the command:

```bash
ss -lunp | grep -E ':(67|547)\s'
```

Sources:
- [Managing networking infrastructure services](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/managing_networking_infrastructure_services/index)
- [How to configure/setup a PXE server on RHEL 10 ?](https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7126352)

Content: Replacing TCP Wrappers in RHEL 8 and 9 Solution Verified - Updated 28 May 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 and 9 firewalld systemd Issue How do I set up access restrictions instead of TCP Wrappers? Do /etc/hosts.allow and /etc/hosts.deny have any use in RHEL 8? /etc/hosts.allow and /etc/hosts.deny are present on RHEL 8 Beta system. They are in the setup-2.12.2-1.el8.noarch RPM. What is the purpose now when tcp_wrappers is no longer available? How can I use the deprecated and removed tcp_wrappers in RHEL 8? Resolution firewalld can be used as a firewall level replacement for TCP Wrappers. This example allows SSH for the 10.1.1.0/24 network and blocks it for all others. firewalld firewalld operates at the firewall layer and can be used to restrict access to a given service. One approach is to create a new firewall zone for the 10.1.1.0/24 network and enable SSH in the new zone. firewalld enables SSH in the default (public) zone by default. First needs to be removed from the default zone: # firewall-cmd --permanent --remove-service=ssh Create the new zone: # firewall-cmd --permanent --new-zone=mysshzone Add the SSH service and the network to the zone. # firewall-cmd --permanent --zone=mysshzone --add-source=10.1.1.0/24# firewall-cmd --permanent --zone=mysshzone --add-service=ssh Then, reload the firewall to make the new configuration active. # firewall-cmd --reload systemd systemd provides per-unit IP address allow/deny filtering. It works with cgroup v2 .For details, see How to allow specific IP addresses or networks to access the systemd managed services Root Cause TCP Wrappers has been marked as deprecated since 2017: RHEL 7.4 Release Notes - Deprecated Functionality "tcp_wrappers deprecated" Further information is given in the upstream Fedora change to remove the feature: Content from fedoraproject.org is not included. Fedora 28 - Changes - Deprecate TCP wrappers Red Hat Bug 1663556 - /etc/hosts.allow and /etc/hosts.deny files contain extremely inaccurate information was been filed to address these files in RHEL 8.

query: How to set up Kea?
response:
To set up Kea on Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, you will primarily configure it to provide DHCP services for IPv4 and/or IPv6 networks. Kea uses separate configuration files and systemd services for DHCPv4 and DHCPv6.

Here's a general outline of the setup process:

### 1. Install Kea

First, install the Kea package:

```bash
dnf install kea
```

### 2. Configure Kea DHCPv4 Server

1.  Edit the DHCPv4 configuration file, `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf`. Replace the example addresses and network details with those appropriate for your network.

    An example configuration for a basic DHCPv4 server might look like this (adjust `interfaces-config`, `subnet`, `pools`, `routers`, and `domain-name-servers` to match your environment):

    ```json
    {
      "Dhcp4": {
        "interfaces-config": {
          "interfaces": [ "enp1s0" ]
        },
        "subnet4": [
          {
            "id": 1,
            "subnet": "192.168.124.0/24",
            "pools": [
              {
                "pool": "192.168.124.100 - 192.168.124.200"
              }
            ],
            "option-data": [
              {
                "space": "dhcp4",
                "name": "routers",
                "code": 3,
                "data": "192.168.124.10"
              },
              {
                "space": "dhcp4",
                "name": "domain-name-servers",
                "code": 6,
                "data": "192.168.124.10"
              }
            ]
          }
        ]
      }
    }
    ```

2.  Verify the syntax of your DHCPv4 configuration:

    ```bash
    kea-dhcp4 -t /etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf
    ```

    If there are errors, correct them before proceeding.

3.  Update `firewalld` rules to allow incoming DHCPv4 traffic:

    ```bash
    firewall-cmd --permanent --add-service=dhcp
    firewall-cmd --reload
    ```

4.  Enable and start the `kea-dhcp4` service:

    ```bash
    systemctl enable --now kea-dhcp4
    ```

### 3. Configure Kea DHCPv6 Server (Optional)

If you are configuring an IPv6 network, follow these steps:

1.  Edit the DHCPv6 configuration file, `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp6.conf`. Replace the example addresses and network details with those appropriate for your IPv6 network.

    An example configuration for a basic DHCPv6 server might look like this (adjust `interfaces-config`, `subnet`, and `pools` to match your environment):

    ```json
    {
      "Dhcp6": {
        "interfaces-config": {
          "interfaces": [ "enp1s0" ]
        },
        "subnet6": [
          {
            "id": 1,
            "subnet": "fd33::/64",
            "interface": "enp1s0",
            "pools": [
              {
                "pool": "fd33::100-fd33::200"
              }
            ]
          }
        ]
      }
    }
    ```

2.  Verify the syntax of your DHCPv6 configuration:

    ```bash
    kea-dhcp6 -t /etc/kea/kea-dhcp6.conf
    ```

    If there are errors, correct them before proceeding.

3.  Update `firewalld` rules to allow incoming DHCPv6 traffic:

    ```bash
    firewall-cmd --permanent --add-service=dhcpv6
    firewall-cmd --reload
    ```

4.  Enable and start the `kea-dhcp6` service:

    ```bash
    systemctl enable --now kea-dhcp6
    ```

### 4. Verification

On a client machine configured to obtain an IP address via DHCP, check if it received an address from your Kea server:

```bash
ip address show <interface>
```

Replace `<interface>` with the client's network interface name (e.g., `enp1s0`).

### Troubleshooting

You can check on which IPv4 and IPv6 addresses Kea is listening with the command:

```bash
ss -lunp | grep -E ':(67|547)\s'
```

Sources:
- [Managing networking infrastructure services](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/managing_networking_infrastructure_services/index)
- [How to configure/setup a PXE server on RHEL 10 ?](https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7126352)

Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL

Content: Replacing TCP Wrappers in RHEL 8 and 9 Solution Verified - Updated 28 May 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 and 9 firewalld systemd Issue How do I set up access restrictions instead of TCP Wrappers? Do /etc/hosts.allow and /etc/hosts.deny have any use in RHEL 8? /etc/hosts.allow and /etc/hosts.deny are present on RHEL 8 Beta system. They are in the setup-2.12.2-1.el8.noarch RPM. What is the purpose now when tcp_wrappers is no longer available? How can I use the deprecated and removed tcp_wrappers in RHEL 8? Resolution firewalld can be used as a firewall level replacement for TCP Wrappers. This example allows SSH for the 10.1.1.0/24 network and blocks it for all others. firewalld firewalld operates at the firewall layer and can be used to restrict access to a given service. One approach is to create a new firewall zone for the 10.1.1.0/24 network and enable SSH in the new zone. firewalld enables SSH in the default (public) zone by default. First needs to be removed from the default zone: # firewall-cmd --permanent --remove-service=ssh Create the new zone: # firewall-cmd --permanent --new-zone=mysshzone Add the SSH service and the network to the zone. # firewall-cmd --permanent --zone=mysshzone --add-source=10.1.1.0/24# firewall-cmd --permanent --zone=mysshzone --add-service=ssh Then, reload the firewall to make the new configuration active. # firewall-cmd --reload systemd systemd provides per-unit IP address allow/deny filtering. It works with cgroup v2 .For details, see How to allow specific IP addresses or networks to access the systemd managed services Root Cause TCP Wrappers has been marked as deprecated since 2017: RHEL 7.4 Release Notes - Deprecated Functionality "tcp_wrappers deprecated" Further information is given in the upstream Fedora change to remove the feature: Content from fedoraproject.org is not included. Fedora 28 - Changes - Deprecate TCP wrappers Red Hat Bug 1663556 - /etc/hosts.allow and /etc/hosts.deny files contain extremely inaccurate information was been filed to address these files in RHEL 8.

query: How to set up Kea?
response:
To set up Kea on Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, you will primarily configure it to provide DHCP services for IPv4 and/or IPv6 networks. Kea uses separate configuration files and systemd services for DHCPv4 and DHCPv6.

Here's a general outline of the setup process:

### 1. Install Kea

First, install the Kea package:

```bash
dnf install kea
```

### 2. Configure Kea DHCPv4 Server

1.  Edit the DHCPv4 configuration file, `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf`. Replace the example addresses and network details with those appropriate for your network.

    An example configuration for a basic DHCPv4 server might look like this (adjust `interfaces-config`, `subnet`, `pools`, `routers`, and `domain-name-servers` to match your environment):

    ```json
    {
      "Dhcp4": {
        "interfaces-config": {
          "interfaces": [ "enp1s0" ]
        },
        "subnet4": [
          {
            "id": 1,
            "subnet": "192.168.124.0/24",
            "pools": [
              {
                "pool": "192.168.124.100 - 192.168.124.200"
              }
            ],
            "option-data": [
              {
                "space": "dhcp4",
                "name": "routers",
                "code": 3,
                "data": "192.168.124.10"
              },
              {
                "space": "dhcp4",
                "name": "domain-name-servers",
                "code": 6,
                "data": "192.168.124.10"
              }
            ]
          }
        ]
      }
    }
    ```

2.  Verify the syntax of your DHCPv4 configuration:

    ```bash
    kea-dhcp4 -t /etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf
    ```

    If there are errors, correct them before proceeding.

3.  Update `firewalld` rules to allow incoming DHCPv4 traffic:

    ```bash
    firewall-cmd --permanent --add-service=dhcp
    firewall-cmd --reload
    ```

4.  Enable and start the `kea-dhcp4` service:

    ```bash
    systemctl enable --now kea-dhcp4
    ```

### 3. Configure Kea DHCPv6 Server (Optional)

If you are configuring an IPv6 network, follow these steps:

1.  Edit the DHCPv6 configuration file, `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp6.conf`. Replace the example addresses and network details with those appropriate for your IPv6 network.

    An example configuration for a basic DHCPv6 server might look like this (adjust `interfaces-config`, `subnet`, and `pools` to match your environment):

    ```json
    {
      "Dhcp6": {
        "interfaces-config": {
          "interfaces": [ "enp1s0" ]
        },
        "subnet6": [
          {
            "id": 1,
            "subnet": "fd33::/64",
            "interface": "enp1s0",
            "pools": [
              {
                "pool": "fd33::100-fd33::200"
              }
            ]
          }
        ]
      }
    }
    ```

2.  Verify the syntax of your DHCPv6 configuration:

    ```bash
    kea-dhcp6 -t /etc/kea/kea-dhcp6.conf
    ```

    If there are errors, correct them before proceeding.

3.  Update `firewalld` rules to allow incoming DHCPv6 traffic:

    ```bash
    firewall-cmd --permanent --add-service=dhcpv6
    firewall-cmd --reload
    ```

4.  Enable and start the `kea-dhcp6` service:

    ```bash
    systemctl enable --now kea-dhcp6
    ```

### 4. Verification

On a client machine configured to obtain an IP address via DHCP, check if it received an address from your Kea server:

```bash
ip address show <interface>
```

Replace `<interface>` with the client's network interface name (e.g., `enp1s0`).

### Troubleshooting

You can check on which IPv4 and IPv6 addresses Kea is listening with the command:

```bash
ss -lunp | grep -E ':(67|547)\s'
```

Sources:
- [Managing networking infrastructure services](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/managing_networking_infrastructure_services/index)
- [How to configure/setup a PXE server on RHEL 10 ?](https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7126352)

Content: Replacing TCP Wrappers in RHEL 8 and 9 Solution Verified - Updated 28 May 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 and 9 firewalld systemd Issue How do I set up access restrictions instead of TCP Wrappers? Do /etc/hosts.allow and /etc/hosts.deny have any use in RHEL 8? /etc/hosts.allow and /etc/hosts.deny are present on RHEL 8 Beta system. They are in the setup-2.12.2-1.el8.noarch RPM. What is the purpose now when tcp_wrappers is no longer available? How can I use the deprecated and removed tcp_wrappers in RHEL 8? Resolution firewalld can be used as a firewall level replacement for TCP Wrappers. This example allows SSH for the 10.1.1.0/24 network and blocks it for all others. firewalld firewalld operates at the firewall layer and can be used to restrict access to a given service. One approach is to create a new firewall zone for the 10.1.1.0/24 network and enable SSH in the new zone. firewalld enables SSH in the default (public) zone by default. First needs to be removed from the default zone: # firewall-cmd --permanent --remove-service=ssh Create the new zone: # firewall-cmd --permanent --new-zone=mysshzone Add the SSH service and the network to the zone. # firewall-cmd --permanent --zone=mysshzone --add-source=10.1.1.0/24# firewall-cmd --permanent --zone=mysshzone --add-service=ssh Then, reload the firewall to make the new configuration active. # firewall-cmd --reload systemd systemd provides per-unit IP address allow/deny filtering. It works with cgroup v2 .For details, see How to allow specific IP addresses or networks to access the systemd managed services Root Cause TCP Wrappers has been marked as deprecated since 2017: RHEL 7.4 Release Notes - Deprecated Functionality "tcp_wrappers deprecated" Further information is given in the upstream Fedora change to remove the feature: Content from fedoraproject.org is not included. Fedora 28 - Changes - Deprecate TCP wrappers Red Hat Bug 1663556 - /etc/hosts.allow and /etc/hosts.deny files contain extremely inaccurate information was been filed to address these files in RHEL 8.

query: How to set up Kea?
response:
To set up Kea on Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, you will primarily configure it to provide DHCP services for IPv4 and/or IPv6 networks. Kea uses separate configuration files and systemd services for DHCPv4 and DHCPv6.

Here's a general outline of the setup process:

### 1. Install Kea

First, install the Kea package:

```bash
dnf install kea
```

### 2. Configure Kea DHCPv4 Server

1.  Edit the DHCPv4 configuration file, `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf`. Replace the example addresses and network details with those appropriate for your network.

    An example configuration for a basic DHCPv4 server might look like this (adjust `interfaces-config`, `subnet`, `pools`, `routers`, and `domain-name-servers` to match your environment):

    ```json
    {
      "Dhcp4": {
        "interfaces-config": {
          "interfaces": [ "enp1s0" ]
        },
        "subnet4": [
          {
            "id": 1,
            "subnet": "192.168.124.0/24",
            "pools": [
              {
                "pool": "192.168.124.100 - 192.168.124.200"
              }
            ],
            "option-data": [
              {
                "space": "dhcp4",
                "name": "routers",
                "code": 3,
                "data": "192.168.124.10"
              },
              {
                "space": "dhcp4",
                "name": "domain-name-servers",
                "code": 6,
                "data": "192.168.124.10"
              }
            ]
          }
        ]
      }
    }
    ```

2.  Verify the syntax of your DHCPv4 configuration:

    ```bash
    kea-dhcp4 -t /etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf
    ```

    If there are errors, correct them before proceeding.

3.  Update `firewalld` rules to allow incoming DHCPv4 traffic:

    ```bash
    firewall-cmd --permanent --add-service=dhcp
    firewall-cmd --reload
    ```

4.  Enable and start the `kea-dhcp4` service:

    ```bash
    systemctl enable --now kea-dhcp4
    ```

### 3. Configure Kea DHCPv6 Server (Optional)

If you are configuring an IPv6 network, follow these steps:

1.  Edit the DHCPv6 configuration file, `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp6.conf`. Replace the example addresses and network details with those appropriate for your IPv6 network.

    An example configuration for a basic DHCPv6 server might look like this (adjust `interfaces-config`, `subnet`, and `pools` to match your environment):

    ```json
    {
      "Dhcp6": {
        "interfaces-config": {
          "interfaces": [ "enp1s0" ]
        },
        "subnet6": [
          {
            "id": 1,
            "subnet": "fd33::/64",
            "interface": "enp1s0",
            "pools": [
              {
                "pool": "fd33::100-fd33::200"
              }
            ]
          }
        ]
      }
    }
    ```

2.  Verify the syntax of your DHCPv6 configuration:

    ```bash
    kea-dhcp6 -t /etc/kea/kea-dhcp6.conf
    ```

    If there are errors, correct them before proceeding.

3.  Update `firewalld` rules to allow incoming DHCPv6 traffic:

    ```bash
    firewall-cmd --permanent --add-service=dhcpv6
    firewall-cmd --reload
    ```

4.  Enable and start the `kea-dhcp6` service:

    ```bash
    systemctl enable --now kea-dhcp6
    ```

### 4. Verification

On a client machine configured to obtain an IP address via DHCP, check if it received an address from your Kea server:

```bash
ip address show <interface>
```

Replace `<interface>` with the client's network interface name (e.g., `enp1s0`).

### Troubleshooting

You can check on which IPv4 and IPv6 addresses Kea is listening with the command:

```bash
ss -lunp | grep -E ':(67|547)\s'
```

Sources:
- [Managing networking infrastructure services](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/managing_networking_infrastructure_services/index)
- [How to configure/setup a PXE server on RHEL 10 ?](https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7126352)

Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL

Content: Replacing TCP Wrappers in RHEL 8 and 9 Solution Verified - Updated 28 May 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 and 9 firewalld systemd Issue How do I set up access restrictions instead of TCP Wrappers? Do /etc/hosts.allow and /etc/hosts.deny have any use in RHEL 8? /etc/hosts.allow and /etc/hosts.deny are present on RHEL 8 Beta system. They are in the setup-2.12.2-1.el8.noarch RPM. What is the purpose now when tcp_wrappers is no longer available? How can I use the deprecated and removed tcp_wrappers in RHEL 8? Resolution firewalld can be used as a firewall level replacement for TCP Wrappers. This example allows SSH for the 10.1.1.0/24 network and blocks it for all others. firewalld firewalld operates at the firewall layer and can be used to restrict access to a given service. One approach is to create a new firewall zone for the 10.1.1.0/24 network and enable SSH in the new zone. firewalld enables SSH in the default (public) zone by default. First needs to be removed from the default zone: # firewall-cmd --permanent --remove-service=ssh Create the new zone: # firewall-cmd --permanent --new-zone=mysshzone Add the SSH service and the network to the zone. # firewall-cmd --permanent --zone=mysshzone --add-source=10.1.1.0/24# firewall-cmd --permanent --zone=mysshzone --add-service=ssh Then, reload the firewall to make the new configuration active. # firewall-cmd --reload systemd systemd provides per-unit IP address allow/deny filtering. It works with cgroup v2 .For details, see How to allow specific IP addresses or networks to access the systemd managed services Root Cause TCP Wrappers has been marked as deprecated since 2017: RHEL 7.4 Release Notes - Deprecated Functionality "tcp_wrappers deprecated" Further information is given in the upstream Fedora change to remove the feature: Content from fedoraproject.org is not included. Fedora 28 - Changes - Deprecate TCP wrappers Red Hat Bug 1663556 - /etc/hosts.allow and /etc/hosts.deny files contain extremely inaccurate information was been filed to address these files in RHEL 8.

query: How do I set up a DHCP server?
response:
To set up a DHCP server on Red Hat Enterprise Linux, you generally follow these steps:

1.  **Install the DHCP server package:**
    The DHCP server functionality is provided by the `dhcp-server` package (or `dhcp` in older versions). You can install it using `yum` or `dnf`.

    ```bash
    sudo dnf install dhcp-server
    ```

2.  **Configure the DHCP server:**
    The main configuration file for the DHCP server is `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf`. When you first install the package, this file is often empty or contains minimal examples. You'll need to edit this file to define your subnets, IP address ranges, DNS servers, and other DHCP options.

    Here is a basic example of what you might include in your `dhcpd.conf` file:

    ```
    # dhcpd.conf

    # Define common options
    option domain-name "example.com";
    option domain-name-servers ns1.example.com, ns2.example.com;
    default-lease-time 600;
    max-lease-time 7200;

    # If this DHCP server is the official DHCP server for the local network, the authoritative directive should be uncommented.
    # authoritative;

    # A slightly different configuration for an internal subnet.
    subnet 192.168.1.0 netmask 255.255.255.0 {
        range 192.168.1.100 192.168.1.200;
        option routers 192.168.1.1;
        option broadcast-address 192.168.1.255;
    }
    ```
    *   `domain-name` and `domain-name-servers`: Specifies the domain name and DNS servers for clients.
    *   `default-lease-time` and `max-lease-time`: Defines the default and maximum lease times for IP addresses in seconds.
    *   `authoritative`: Uncomment this if your DHCP server is the only one on the network.
    *   `subnet`: Defines the network segment for which the DHCP server will provide IP addresses.
    *   `range`: Specifies the pool of IP addresses that the DHCP server can assign.
    *   `option routers`: Sets the default gateway for clients.
    *   `option broadcast-address`: Sets the broadcast address for the subnet.

    After making changes, ensure the configuration file is syntactically correct:
    ```bash
    sudo dhcpd -t -cf /etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf
    ```

3.  **Configure the network interface:**
    You need to ensure that the DHCP server listens on the correct network interface. Edit the `/etc/sysconfig/dhcpd` file (if it exists) or configure the `dhcpd` service to start on the desired interface. For systemd, you might need to enable it for specific interfaces.

    For example, to specify an interface, you can edit `/etc/sysconfig/dhcpd` and add:
    ```
    DHCPDARGS=eth0
    ```
    Replace `eth0` with your actual network interface.

4.  **Start and enable the DHCP service:**
    ```bash
    sudo systemctl start dhcpd
    sudo systemctl enable dhcpd
    ```

5.  **Configure firewall rules:**
    Allow DHCP traffic through your firewall. DHCP uses UDP ports 67 (server) and 68 (client).

    ```bash
    sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=dhcp --permanent
    sudo firewall-cmd --reload
    ```

The provided documentation is for RHEL 5, but the fundamental concepts and command-line tools for setting up a DHCP server remain largely similar across RHEL versions. Always refer to the official Red Hat documentation for your specific RHEL version for the most accurate and up-to-date instructions.

**Sources:**
- [title](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/5/html/deployment_guide/s1-dhcp-configuring-server)
- [title](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/5/html/deployment_guide/sect-configuring_a_multihomed_dhcp_server)

Content: Is "nfs_export_all_rw" boolean available in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6.4? Solution Verified - Updated 6 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6.4 selinux-policy-3.7.19-195.el6 and higher. Issue Unable to set "nfs_export_all_rw" boolean value for nfs using setsebool in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6.4 While trying to set a boolean value for nfs using setsebool, following error is observed. # setsebool -P nfs_export_all_rw onlibsemanage.dbase_llist_set: record not found in the database (No such file or directory) Resolution "nfs_export_all_rw" boolean is no longer needed RHEL6.4 onwards and is deprecated in following bugzilla This content is not included. ... Root Cause SELinux boolean "nfs_export_all_rw" for NFS failed to prevent a NFS client from accessing a share. Consequently, the NFS client could mount a NFS share and read or write files. Because the NFS server runs as a kernel process, the "nfs_export_all_rw" boolean variable is no longer needed and has been removed from the policy. Diagnostic Steps The removal of boolean can be verified by below command :- # getsebool -a | grep nfs_export_all_rw# This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

query: How do I set up a DHCP server?
response:
To set up a DHCP server on Red Hat Enterprise Linux, you generally follow these steps:

1.  **Install the DHCP server package:**
    The DHCP server functionality is provided by the `dhcp-server` package (or `dhcp` in older versions). You can install it using `yum` or `dnf`.

    ```bash
    sudo dnf install dhcp-server
    ```

2.  **Configure the DHCP server:**
    The main configuration file for the DHCP server is `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf`. When you first install the package, this file is often empty or contains minimal examples. You'll need to edit this file to define your subnets, IP address ranges, DNS servers, and other DHCP options.

    Here is a basic example of what you might include in your `dhcpd.conf` file:

    ```
    # dhcpd.conf

    # Define common options
    option domain-name "example.com";
    option domain-name-servers ns1.example.com, ns2.example.com;
    default-lease-time 600;
    max-lease-time 7200;

    # If this DHCP server is the official DHCP server for the local network, the authoritative directive should be uncommented.
    # authoritative;

    # A slightly different configuration for an internal subnet.
    subnet 192.168.1.0 netmask 255.255.255.0 {
        range 192.168.1.100 192.168.1.200;
        option routers 192.168.1.1;
        option broadcast-address 192.168.1.255;
    }
    ```
    *   `domain-name` and `domain-name-servers`: Specifies the domain name and DNS servers for clients.
    *   `default-lease-time` and `max-lease-time`: Defines the default and maximum lease times for IP addresses in seconds.
    *   `authoritative`: Uncomment this if your DHCP server is the only one on the network.
    *   `subnet`: Defines the network segment for which the DHCP server will provide IP addresses.
    *   `range`: Specifies the pool of IP addresses that the DHCP server can assign.
    *   `option routers`: Sets the default gateway for clients.
    *   `option broadcast-address`: Sets the broadcast address for the subnet.

    After making changes, ensure the configuration file is syntactically correct:
    ```bash
    sudo dhcpd -t -cf /etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf
    ```

3.  **Configure the network interface:**
    You need to ensure that the DHCP server listens on the correct network interface. Edit the `/etc/sysconfig/dhcpd` file (if it exists) or configure the `dhcpd` service to start on the desired interface. For systemd, you might need to enable it for specific interfaces.

    For example, to specify an interface, you can edit `/etc/sysconfig/dhcpd` and add:
    ```
    DHCPDARGS=eth0
    ```
    Replace `eth0` with your actual network interface.

4.  **Start and enable the DHCP service:**
    ```bash
    sudo systemctl start dhcpd
    sudo systemctl enable dhcpd
    ```

5.  **Configure firewall rules:**
    Allow DHCP traffic through your firewall. DHCP uses UDP ports 67 (server) and 68 (client).

    ```bash
    sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=dhcp --permanent
    sudo firewall-cmd --reload
    ```

The provided documentation is for RHEL 5, but the fundamental concepts and command-line tools for setting up a DHCP server remain largely similar across RHEL versions. Always refer to the official Red Hat documentation for your specific RHEL version for the most accurate and up-to-date instructions.

**Sources:**
- [title](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/5/html/deployment_guide/s1-dhcp-configuring-server)
- [title](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/5/html/deployment_guide/sect-configuring_a_multihomed_dhcp_server)

Content: Is "nfs_export_all_rw" boolean available in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6.4? Solution Verified - Updated 6 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6.4 selinux-policy-3.7.19-195.el6 and higher. Issue Unable to set "nfs_export_all_rw" boolean value for nfs using setsebool in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6.4 While trying to set a boolean value for nfs using setsebool, following error is observed. # setsebool -P nfs_export_all_rw onlibsemanage.dbase_llist_set: record not found in the database (No such file or directory) Resolution "nfs_export_all_rw" boolean is no longer needed RHEL6.4 onwards and is deprecated in following bugzilla This content is not included. ... Root Cause SELinux boolean "nfs_export_all_rw" for NFS failed to prevent a NFS client from accessing a share. Consequently, the NFS client could mount a NFS share and read or write files. Because the NFS server runs as a kernel process, the "nfs_export_all_rw" boolean variable is no longer needed and has been removed from the policy. Diagnostic Steps The removal of boolean can be verified by below command :- # getsebool -a | grep nfs_export_all_rw# This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.
